questions.description: The Defamation Act 2009 was never fit for purpose and requires significant revision if it is to meet the challenges of a…
questions.text: The Defamation Act 2009 was never fit for purpose and requires significant revision if it is to meet the challenges of a changing society. Critically discuss this statement paying particular attention to the deficiencies of the Act and recent suggestions for reform.

questions.description: Tom agreed to meet his wife, Rachel, for lunch in town. As Tom is walking down the street towards the restaurant where t…
questions.text: Tom agreed to meet his wife, Rachel, for lunch in town. As Tom is walking down the street towards the restaurant where they are to meet, he spots Rachel in the distance. He begins to wave at her when he hears a strange sound and looks up. He then sees a large neon sign suddenly, and without warning, fall from the third floor of the building overhead Rachel. Tom shouts a warning to Rachel but knows it is too late. He closes his eyes and drops to his knees convinced Rachel has been killed when he hears the sign crash onto the pavement. People are screaming and crying in the street and all he can do is lie on the street, wailing and sick at the thought he has lost his wife. He cannot picture life without her. In shock he is completely disoriented and is taken by ambulance to hospital. While waiting on a trolley in the Emergency Room, he sees Rachel walking towards him. Convinced that he is seeing her ghost who blames him for not saving her, he tries to flee and falls off the trolley, fracturing his ankle. In fact, Rachel avoided the falling sign and was unharmed in the incident. However, Tom is now on significant medication for these traumatic events, in particular the ghostly apparition he thought he saw. Advise Tom if he has any action in tort against the owners of the building for what he has suffered.

questions.description: In a well to do suburban neighbourhood, a local supermarket has recently installed a large bottle deposit return recycli…
questions.text: In a well to do suburban neighbourhood, a local supermarket has recently installed a large bottle deposit return recycling machine in their car park. This machine accepts recyclable cans and plastic bottles on which a deposit has been paid when purchased. When they are returned to the machine, the person is given a voucher for the amount of the deposit which can then be spent in store. It has been in operation for a number of months. However, in recent times, the machine is either out of order or rejecting cans. As a result people are just leaving them next to the machine. The store owners decided to locate small bins next to the machine for cans which were not accepted by the machine. It appears that some people have begun to use these bins as a general can recycling drop off point as well as for rejected cans. Local residents in the housing estates surrounding the supermarket are upset at a number of issues. First, many of the cans still contain sugary liquids which are attracting flies and wasps. Second there is a strong smell from these cans and finally some children are using the cans to play ‘football’ in the car park when the supermarket is closed, creating a racket late at night. Advise local residents if they have any action in tort law with respect to this situation.

questions.description: Critically discuss the doctrine of res ipsa locquitur in Irish law and analyse whether it retains any usefulness in mode…
questions.text: Critically discuss the doctrine of res ipsa locquitur in Irish law and analyse whether it retains any usefulness in modern tort law.

questions.description: Megan is a patient in Mount Mercy Hospital. She undergoes surgery to correct a herniated disc in her spine. Her surgeon …
questions.text: Megan is a patient in Mount Mercy Hospital. She undergoes surgery to correct a herniated disc in her spine. Her surgeon is Alan, with over. 30 years’ experience. However, Alan has been having some personal issues and was intoxicated during the surgery. He makes several mistakes resulting in complications for Megan and the hospital has accepted liability. Following the surgery, in 90% of cases Megan would have been expected to be off work for 6 weeks, suffering moderate to mild discomfort during that period but would be expected to resume a normal life and continue without any issues. For about 10% of cases, patients may continue to suffer chronic pain of a mild nature that might need occasional pain medication when it becomes severe. For Megan following the operation she now suffers significant chronic pain, which no medication appears to alleviate. She is also abnormally anxious about further injuring her back and so has assumed a sedentary lifestyle, gaining weight and is now diabetic. She has incurred about €20,000 on alterations to her home, many of which were unnecessary but which mentally Megan desired to have done. Also, Megan can never see herself going under a surgery again as she is terrified that all surgeons are drunk. Advise Megan the principles underlying an award of damages in tort law arising from these events.

questions.description: Mary, a homeowner, asked Brendan, a retired builder, to erect a solid boundary wall to divide her property from that of …
questions.text: Mary, a homeowner, asked Brendan, a retired builder, to erect a solid boundary wall to divide her property from that of her neighbour, Alison. Mary tells Brendan that there has been tension with Alison and she wants to ensure that the wall is correctly put up. Where Mary lives is subject to high winds that have often resulted in items from Mary’s garden blowing around causing damage to her garden. Mary wants the wall to stop this from happening. Brendan says he will build it as strong as he can but with climate change the storms are getting ever stronger. When the wall is completed a storm hits. The wall collapses and destroys a shed belonging to Alison. Mary believes Brendan should pay for the damage but he says that no structure can ever be fully secured from ‘unusual’ weather events but it was built to the highest standards in the industry. On inspection, the wall had pillars every 4 metres although there is evidence that a common practice in construction is to have pillars every 2.5 metres unless site conditions prevent that being possible. Brendan says that he had been in the industry for 40 years and that most walls are built with pillars every 4 metres and that no one puts pillars only 2.5 metres apart. Advise Alison if she has an action in negligence against Brendan.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law relating to liability for defective products and whether the current framework provides an ef…
questions.text: Critically discuss the law relating to liability for defective products and whether the current framework provides an effective and efficient mechanism for compensation for harms caused to plaintiffs.

questions.description: Laura and Lucy work together in an office. In the beginning, they were very friendly but a dispute arose with respect to…
questions.text: Laura and Lucy work together in an office. In the beginning, they were very friendly but a dispute arose with respect to the organisation of a conference where each blamed the other for things that went wrong. Both were told off by their manager for what had happened but each continued to believe that the other was solely to blame for what went wrong. Over the following weeks, their resentment simmered and in the end they were unable to talk to each other. Oonagh, their line manager called them to a meeting in the Boardroom and explained that this needed to be sorted. Both Lucy and Laura just stared at each other. Neither would say anything. Suddenly Oonagh slammed her fist on the table and shouted at them that no one would leave the room until the issues had been resolved. She went over to the door and appeared to lock it. This did not improve the atmosphere in the room at all and in fact made it worse. Laura got a text message and stood up, telling Oonagh that she had to leave as her daughter was sick and needed to be taken home from school. As she got up, Lucy stood up and blocked her path, saying “I have had enough of this – you are going nowhere until this is sorted. Sit down or I will make you!” Laura was now scared and sat down. With that, Oonagh threw her notepad across the table, knocking over a glass of water onto Laura. Oonagh then stormed out of the room, which was not locked. Advise Laura with respect to any tort action she may have concerning these events.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law with regard to establishing that a duty of care exists in tort actions, paying particular att…
questions.text: Critically discuss the law with regard to establishing that a duty of care exists in tort actions, paying particular attention to the development from Donoghue v Stevenson to date.

questions.description: In the small Cork-knit village of Ballyvogue, a local free parish newsletter called "The Ballyvogue Bugle" is produced b…
questions.text: In the small Cork-knit village of Ballyvogue, a local free parish newsletter called "The Ballyvogue Bugle" is produced by residents of the village. The newsletter provides news, recipes and stories from within the village. The restaurant's staff felt anonymous sources from within the restaurant, a brand-new eatery writes the articles. The restaurant review published in the Bugle gave the restaurant terrible reviews. The newsletter did not name the author of the articles. David, the owner of Gillan's, does some research online and finds the restaurant review has been copied from the newsletter and posted on a restaurant review website where the article has since gone viral. David now seeks compensation for the damage caused to Gillan. Gillan argues she cannot be held responsible for something she did not write or post online. She further states that she cannot retract something that she is not responsible for. Advise David as to his legal position under the law of tort.

questions.description: Harry owned a semi-detached house in a large estate that he rented to Alison. Almost immediately after Alison moved in, …
questions.text: Harry owned a semi-detached house in a large estate that he rented to Alison. Almost immediately after Alison moved in, she installed a large heat pump that was extremely noisy. The noise was being generated to make a really loud noise during the winter months. Aoife complained to Alison many times about the noise but Alison thought Aoife was being overly sensitive and continued. As time went on, Aoife found she could not sleep at night. She also noticed that her pet dogs were becoming very distressed. Despite paying rent, Aoife went to the landlord Harry but he was not interested in causing her any problems, and he issued an order for eviction. Nothing happened as it was put on hold and stopped paying rent. Despite the order for eviction, Aoife remained in possession but now the noise of the heat pump was so bad, getting worse. Finally, Aoife could stand it no more and sought legal advice as to whether she could have Alison's heat pump permanently switched off. Advise Aoife as to any legal action that might be available to her under the law of tort.

questions.description: Critically discuss the calculation of damages in tort actions, in particular the assessment of future losses.
questions.text: Critically discuss the calculation of damages in tort actions, in particular the assessment of future losses.

questions.description: In a rugby match between a local secondary school, Liam, a player on the home team, was tackled aggressively by Scott, a…
questions.text: In a rugby match between a local secondary school, Liam, a player on the home team, was tackled aggressively by Scott, a player on the away team. Scott's tackle was reckless and dangerous, causing Liam to suffer a serious knee injury. The referee did not penalise Scott for the tackle. Liam had to be taken off the field and subsequently underwent surgery. His recovery was expected to take 8-10 months. Scott claimed it was a legitimate tackle and he should not be held liable for injuries sustained during a contact sport. The school's sports teacher had not adequately explained the rules and safety procedures to Scott before the match. Lara, a spectator watching the match, was hit by a ball that went out of play. She suffered a broken nose. The school had not installed any safety netting or barriers around the pitch to protect spectators. Advise Liam and Lara as to their legal position under the law of tort.

questions.description: At a construction site for a new hospital, Luke was concluding a series of power tests along various electrical lines. T…
questions.text: At a construction site for a new hospital, Luke was concluding a series of power tests along various electrical lines. The main contractor, BIFF, had hired several workers including Luke to do work on an occupation basis. This testing is a standard safety procedure before the power would be switched on permanently. Luke was testing the power lines using electrical equipment, when another worker accidentally turned on one of the power switches. The power surged through the line that Luke was testing. The switch was poorly labeled and had a defective guard. On the explosion, it was found that Luke was walking to get to his work. He had to walk about 10 minutes to get to the site from the car park, along a path alongside the power lines. It would be easy to see that the path was always wet and slippery and was extremely dangerous. Bored with the waiting, one of the other workers on site picked up a nail gun and started firing nails at targets. Unfortunately, he hit Luke in the leg and ankle, seriously injuring him. Luke was not authorised to have a nail gun. BIFF are refusing any liability for the loss of power, saying Luke was not authorised to have a nail gun. Advise BIFF as to their legal position under the law of tort.

questions.description: In the world of luxury fashion, two high-end shoe brands, "Looboos" (an established firm over 100 years old) and "Loofau…
questions.text: In the world of luxury fashion, two high-end shoe brands, "Looboos" (an established firm over 100 years old) and "Loofaus" (a new firm barely in existence for 10 years) have been competing for market share. Looboos, with its distinctive logo and elegant designs, has a significant reputation among consumers, although sales have flatlined as they are seen as a little dated. Recently, Loofaus launched a new shoe line, with a logo and packaging strikingly similar to Looboos and even using the tagline "Picking up where Looboos left off." Loofaus claim they are paying homage to Looboos' legacy. Looboos complains that Loofaus is trying to piggyback on their brand history and that they are deliberately setting themselves up to look as if they are an offshoot of Looboos. Loofaus claim the exact opposite, arguing that their whole approach is to create a feeling within the market that Looboos is an outdated brand that is being replaced by their modern, chic shoe line. They claim that people who wear their shoes would not be seen dead in Looboos. Critically discuss any potential action for Looboos in the law of tort.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law regarding fatal injuries in the law of tort paying particular attention to statutory authorit…
questions.text: Critically discuss the law regarding fatal injuries in the law of tort paying particular attention to statutory authority and judicial precedent.

questions.description: John worked for VultureWings DAC, a low cost airline, as an aircraft maintenance specialist. John was working on a Vultu…
questions.text: John worked for VultureWings DAC, a low cost airline, as an aircraft maintenance specialist. John was working on a VultureWings aircraft as it was at the gate preparing to load passengers for an outgoing flight. John found himself working on the hydraulic system of the wing. It was a difficult job particularly as VultureWings did not have the necessary equipment that John could work on it from the ground. Although the maintenance workers had all been trained to not work on any aircraft without the proper equipment, John was conscious this would be the fourth plane which was delayed due to maintenance issues. As a result he decided to climb onto the wing to undertake the repairs. Jennifer, a passenger on that flight, had arrived early and was sitting at the gate. She could see John working on the aircraft and found it hugely interesting. At one point, she caught John’s eye and waved at him. John looked up and waved back. In doing this he lost his balance and found himself sliding off the wing unable to stop his fall. Jennifer could not bring herself to look and could only imagine the terrible consequences of John’s fall, certain that he was dead. In fact John was tethered to the wing and so never fell to the ground and was in fact unhurt. But Jennifer could not shake the image from her mind and now finds herself with a debilitating fear of flying. Advise VultureWings on any potential tort liability to Jennifer that might arise in this situation.

questions.description: In tort law the limitation periods within which an action must be taken have been so amended that far from providing for…
questions.text: In tort law the limitation periods within which an action must be taken have been so amended that far from providing for certainty in litigation, limitation periods have now become a source of increased uncertainty. Critically discuss this statement in light of the current rules on limitation of actions in the law of tort

questions.description: Eamonn has become increasingly worried about the changing environment and, in particular, the potential for global warmi…
questions.text: Eamonn has become increasingly worried about the changing environment and, in particular, the potential for global warming which could lead to life threatening water shortages in the country. With that in mind, he has decided to make certain changes on his property that will assist his neighbours in the event of any catastrophic environmental disaster. Eamonn has installed a rain water harvesting system all around his house and sheds. With this system, all the rain that falls, and which would normally just be allowed run off the rooves of the house and sheds, is gathered together in a single large water treatment container where it is purified so that it can be safely drunk by humans. A series of pipes then channels the water to underground storage tanks. There is an inbuilt safety mechanism which means that if the storage tanks become too full, a controlled amount of drinking water is released from the tanks so as to avoid any risk of flooding any of the surrounding neighbours if the water is released too quickly. The system has worked exceptionally well but in recent days, the level of rain experienced has exceeded all known records by a huge amount, and far beyond what the system is designed to deal with. Accordingly, the tanks release an uncontrolled amount of water in the surrounding neighbourhood, causing considerable flooding. Eamonn is convinced that he is not to blame, as he only collected the rain which fell naturally and so the flooding is not his fault. Advise Eamonn as to whether he has any potential liability in tort law.

questions.description: Critically examine the defence of consent or waiver under s.34(1)(b) of the Civil Liability Act, 1961
questions.text: Critically examine the defence of consent or waiver under s.34(1)(b) of the Civil Liability Act, 1961

questions.description: Nicola is famous on a video sharing website where she regularly reviews products that she buys. The reviews include demo…
questions.text: Nicola is famous on a video sharing website where she regularly reviews products that she buys. The reviews include demonstrating how the product is used, special tricks that can be used to further enhance the utility of the item and her overall rating of the item in terms of value for money, reliability etc. Nicola has become something of a celebrity on the website with over 100,000 followers. As a result, she has been noticed by major companies who now specifically send her free products to test and trial on her video, hoping to use her reviews to profile their products to the general public, particularly the younger demographic who use these channels. The companies do not pay her for this, but she does get to keep all the products she receives, whether or not she reviews them. A while back, Nicola received a microwave from Siny Electronics to review. Eventually in the last few days she decided that she would review the item. Under pressure of time, Nicola did not bother to read the instruction booklet and presumed that it looked like a microwave that she had previously reviewed. As a result, on her video she said that the great thing about it was that you could reheat metal takeaway containers directly in the microwave. In fact this was wrong. However, Carmel did as Nicola suggested causing her main house fuse to blow and necessitating expensive electrical repairs to her house. Carmel seeks your advice as to whether Siny Electronics have any liability in the law of tort for Nicola’s review.

questions.description: Critically discuss whether the doctrine of res ipsa locquitur in Irish tort law should be abolished or not.
questions.text: Critically discuss whether the doctrine of res ipsa locquitur in Irish tort law should be abolished or not.

questions.description: Bill had been friendly with Donal, but over time the relationship had soured for no obvious reason. Donal was upset and …
questions.text: Bill had been friendly with Donal, but over time the relationship had soured for no obvious reason. Donal was upset and one evening, he called into Bill’s house as he wanted to clear the air between them. Bill said that he would make Donal coffee. But Donal said he had changed his mind and was going home. Bill then went to the front door and locked it, saying “No one leaves until we have had our coffee.” Bill went to the kitchen and came back with the coffee. He gave one to Donal saying “I made this one especially for you. Be sure to drink it all.” Donal replied that it was getting late and he wanted to go home but Bill said no one was leaving until they had drank their coffee. When Donal finished his coffee he stood up to leave. Bill stood up as well and said “That coffee was poisoned. You will be dead within 24 hours, so you are now free to go. There is no cure. Goodbye!” Donal fled the house and drove straight to the hospital where his stomach was pumped but in fact the coffee had not been poisoned. Advise Donal as to any potential claims in tort [only] that he might have against Bill in these circumstances

questions.description: John purchased the latest electric vehicle, an SUV which operated exclusively on battery power. However, he was continua…
questions.text: John purchased the latest electric vehicle, an SUV which operated exclusively on battery power. However, he was continually disappointed with the range of the vehicle as it seldom travelled more than 50% of the distance he had been promised when buying it. As a result he took it back to the dealer who said that they would run some tests on it to see what the issue was. As it was undergoing testing, Sam, a mechanic at the dealership thought that he could smell burning but could not see any smoke or flame so dismissed it. He then connected the vehicle to a supercharging point and began to pump in more charge into the battery. No matter how long it was connected to the charger the battery never appeared to get fully charged. Sam took the vehicle out for a test drive. On that drive the car burst into flames. Sam jumped from the vehicle but had made no attempt to put on the handbrake. As a result the vehicle rolled down an incline still on fire. Some of the sparks from the vehicle landed close to the pram that Mary had her baby in. Mary, seeing the sparks, grabbed the baby from the pram and attempted to run as far away as she could but tripped over a piece of broken pavement about 10 metres from the pram, breaking her leg. On examination, it appears that John had watched an online video which showed how to extend the range of an electric vehicle by keeping the battery pack warmer through a small heating device which he had attached to the car. Advise Mary as to who is the cause of her broken leg under the law of tort.

questions.description: Jacqueline owns a small country hotel that has only just re-opened after the pandemic. Business is slow and with an impe…
questions.text: Jacqueline owns a small country hotel that has only just re-opened after the pandemic. Business is slow and with an impending cost of living crisis, she is concerned that she may have to close permanently. Fred, who owns a plot of land next to her hotel, has begun organising open air musical events which attract large crowds. At first, Jacqueline hopes that those going to the events will come to her hotel and either stay the night or at least buy drinks from the bar. But this does not happen, although she does find that many of those attending the event are spilling out from Fred’s field and partying on her land. Jacqueline then decides to put up signs stating: trespassers will be prosecuted. However, she does not up any fencing on the land itself to stop it from happening. Instead, she sets up a van on the dividing line between her and Fred’s land which sells drinks and food and carries the name of the hotel. She finds that many people think they can use the restrooms of her hotel, but she turns them away and reminds them it is private property, and they need to leave. One night, Sally was attending one of the events at Fred’s. Having bought some drink at the van, she walked up to the hotel seeking to use the restroom, but Jacqueline turned her away saying she was a trespasser. On the way back, Sally saw a pond just outside the hotel and decided it would be fun to jump into it. Unfortunately, someone had thrown rubbish into the pond which was not visible in the dark. When Sally jumped in, her foot got caught in the rubbish and broke her leg. Advise Jacqueline of her legal position with respect to these events in the law of tort.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law relating to defective products and whether the current legal framework provides a suitable re…
questions.text: Critically discuss the law relating to defective products and whether the current legal framework provides a suitable response in the modern world.

questions.description: Alan owns a bakery that makes a product called The Good Kake. It has been on sale for four years and has established a g…
questions.text: Alan owns a bakery that makes a product called The Good Kake. It has been on sale for four years and has established a good-sized market with growing sales. Alan is very health conscious and has devised a cake made exclusively from healthy ingredients that are believed to lead to a healthier outcome and a longer life. On the wrapping, Alan goes through the arguments as to why one should eat his product. “If you want a long life and to be as active as you can until the end, eating healthy will provide that. This Kake, made from healthy natural kale and other produce of the earth, untainted by artificial colours and sweeteners, can be a part of the balanced diet needed to extend the quality and length of our lives. Should be eaten as part of an overall organic healthy diet.” Christina has begun selling a similar looking product in similar almost identical wrapping called Not the Good Kake! The wrapping replicates almost exactly that of the Good Kake. On the wrapping there is a similar list of arguments to that of the Good Kake, but it is in fact a counterargument, stating that an enjoyable life is better than a long life and how important it is to enjoy life rather than be in misery extending it through suffering tasteless food. Alan has noted that his sales, which had been booming have stabilised and even dropped a little since Not the Good Kake came on the market. In the meantime, Not the Good Kake has had a stunning sales success. Alan believes that people are confusing his product with hers. Advise Alan what action, if any, he might have against Christina in the law of tort.

questions.description: Amy works in a supermarket that opens 24 hours a day. She mostly takes the night shift as it pays better even though the…
questions.text: Amy works in a supermarket that opens 24 hours a day. She mostly takes the night shift as it pays better even though the hours are unsocial. During the summer months it is not too bad but as winter draws in, the long dark nights begin to take a toll and Amy is increasingly tired at the end of her shift. When leaving work, Amy is required to clock out and leave by a back alley where the rubbish bins are kept and make her way around to the front where her car is parked. As a result of the rising cost of electricity, management have decided to control their use of electricity and have taken a number of measures to reduce electricity usage. One is to replace the lighting where Amy has to leave sensor lighting only coming on when someone is in the alley. The other is to reduce the intensity of the lighting in the car park to a very low background level but which provided no real visibility. Unfortunately, the sensor lighting is slow to operate, and Amy often finds that she trips over things in the dark before the lights come on. She has complained to management who said they would look into it but nothing has happened. One night, as Amy is leaving, she trips over a crate and injured her ankle before the lights came on. Amy found it difficult to walk but continued to the front where her car park was parked. In the dark, she thought she could hear some muffled voices but continued as best she could. Suddenly from the darkness two youths attacked her and stole her handbag. Advise Amy as to what her legal position in the law of tort is with respect to her employers.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law relating to the impact of death in a tort action and suggest what, if any, reforms you think …
questions.text: Critically discuss the law relating to the impact of death in a tort action and suggest what, if any, reforms you think might be necessary.

questions.description: Marion is a surgeon working at First Memorial Hospital. Following extensive consultations, Marion was to undertake a com…
questions.text: Marion is a surgeon working at First Memorial Hospital. Following extensive consultations, Marion was to undertake a complicated surgery on Dave to remove some possibly cancerous cells. Her assessment at the time was that she would use the hospital’s approved operating procedure for those sorts of cases, although she was aware that there was new research on these procedures. This research argued for a new alternative approach which produced better outcomes, and which was safer than that approved by the hospital. In fact, many hospitals around the world had incorporated this new procedure into their approved methods. While operating on Dave, she realised that he would be an ideal candidate for the new procedure that she was well able to undertake. In fact, she considered that it carried less risk than the approved method in this case. However, she was uncomfortable with departing from the hospital approved procedure in any situation, feeling that she would be better protected by staying within the rules, so she stuck to the approved procedure. Following the operation, Dave suffered some complications that could have been avoided had Marion followed the new procedure. The guidelines for procedures at the hospital stated that “a surgeon should operate within hospital approved guidelines unless using their professional judgement they form the opinion that a recognised alternative can be used for a better patient outcome.” Advise Marion if, under the law of tort, she had breached her duty of care to Dave in these circumstances.

questions.description: Harry was giving a lecture on Greek mythology at a local college to a class of about 20 students. During the course of t…
questions.text:
Harry was giving a lecture on Greek mythology at a local college to a class of about 20 students. During the course of the lecture, an emergency siren activated in the class. Harry then proceeded to look at a text message on his phone. He then told the class that the college had informed him that someone had been seen on the campus with a gun and that an emergency response was being initiated. Harry told the class that the procedure was to lock the door to the room and for everyone to remain in the class but to sit under their desks or to take cover in, behind or under something. People were naturally panicked and began running around the place, although most followed Harry’s orders and got under their desks. Harry went and locked the door and switched out the lights. At that point, Robin walked towards the door, but Harry told her that was too dangerous and for her to get under a desk and wait. Robin screamed that she needed to get home to her family as she had just received a text that her father had a heart attack. Harry was however adamant that no one could leave. Robin then returned to sit at her desk. When told by Harry to get under it, Robin said that she was not afraid of anyone, and they could “bring it on”. Harry said she was putting everyone in danger and pulled her off the chair and pushed her under the desk. 
A few minutes later, Harry announced that it had been a drill to test the readiness of the college to deal with an emergency. Advise Robin if, under the law of tort, she has any action against Harry for his actions.

questions.description: Establishing a duty of care owed by the defendant has now been largely settled into specific categories beyond which the…
questions.text: Establishing a duty of care owed by the defendant has now been largely settled into specific categories beyond which the courts are reluctant to move. Critically discuss this statement with reference to the jurisprudence.

questions.description: The role of fault in the allocation of tort liability has outlived its usefulness in the modern world, particularly with…
questions.text: The role of fault in the allocation of tort liability has outlived its usefulness in the modern world, particularly with new emerging technologies. Critically discuss this statement in light of the role and function of tort law in the modern legal system.

questions.description: Sam was part of a protest walking down the main street. He was protesting the continuation of the mandatory requirement …
questions.text: Sam was part of a protest walking down the main street. He was protesting the continuation of the mandatory requirement to wear facemasks in certain situations. He felt this was an affront to the freedom which his forebears had fought and died for. The protest was peaceful although none of the protestors were wearing facemasks. However, Sam’s protestors were shortly joined by another group of lawful protestors who were expressing the opposite opinion: they wanted mandatory wearing of facemasks in all situations where there were a large number of people. One of these protestors, Diane, who was wearing a disposable mask began harassing Sam, shouting at him that he needed to believe in the science, that no one was taking away his freedoms and so on. She also shouted that it was people like him who had caused the death of her grandfather from Covid. Sam just kept singing the national anthem at her. As she became angrier, her shouting became louder and at that point her own face mask broke and fell to the ground. Seeing his chance, Sam turned to Diane and sang directly in her face. When he had finished, he told her that he had Covid symptoms but was not going to get tested but he hoped that he did have it. He finished by saying “with luck, you will catch it as well and see it for the fake news that it is!” Diane was hysterical at this and ran away. Advise Diane if she has any action in tort against Sam on these facts.

questions.description: Edwina is an environmentalist who lives in a remote part of the country where she has decided to try to live a minimalis…
questions.text: Edwina is an environmentalist who lives in a remote part of the country where she has decided to try to live a minimalist lifestyle. She abhors any use of fossil fuels and now wants to disconnect from the electricity network because she believes it relies too heavily on non-renewable energy sources. Instead, she has decided to generate her own electricity using wind power. As a result of this, Edwina has installed a mini windmill in her back garden. To cater for those periods where this is insufficient to provide for her needs, she has a diesel generator which automatically kicks in to provide electricity. She hopes however that this will only be used as a last resort. Unfortunately, climate change has meant that where Edwina lives the wind either blows very strongly or not at all. This has meant that the diesel generator is now being used for a longer period of time to cover Edwina’s electricity needs. John, her next-door neighbor, is complaining about the noise that the diesel generator makes and also that there is a significant diesel smell from the generator when it is in operation. So much so that he finds it difficult to sleep and feels he cannot use his garden when it is on due to the smell it gives off. In addition, during high winds, John believes that the windmill causes a vibrational hum in John’s house. Advise John if he has any action in nuisance under tort law in these circumstances.

questions.description: Vincent loves vintage cars and has recently restored a 1969 Caprica to pristine condition. Many of the original parts we…
questions.text: Vincent loves vintage cars and has recently restored a 1969 Caprica to pristine condition. Many of the original parts were not available and he had to have them made specifically to order. For that he found Maurice, a supplier who, when given the drawing of the required part, would make them in his engineering workshop. One day Vincent was out driving his 1969 Caprica downtown. In front of him was Joshua, a rival vintage car restorer who was envious of Vincent’s Caprica restoration. Seeking to give Vincent a fright, Joshua stopped his car suddenly and without warning. Unfortunately, Joshua’s brake lights were not working, and Vincent pressed the brakes hard but too late. He crashed into the back of Joshua’s car. As a result of the impact, a pipe taking petrol from the petrol tank to the engine became dislodged. That pipe had been made by Maurice by copying the old part that Vincent had supplied. In fact that pipe had been made by the previous owner and was entirely unsuitable for that purpose. As the pipe dislodged, petrol spilled from the Caprica and seeped underneath several parked cars. Meghan, who was watching the events unfold, decided she would see if either Vincent or Joshua needed help. As she began to run to the incident, she threw her cigarette on the ground unaware that the petrol had been leaking. The cigarette set light to the petrol and destroyed the parked vehicles. Advise Maurice as to who is the cause of the damage to the parked cars under the law of tort.

questions.description: Critically discuss the reform of defamation law paying particular attention to the need to protect the reputation of pla…
questions.text: Critically discuss the reform of defamation law paying particular attention to the need to protect the reputation of plaintiffs.

questions.description: Brendan was a yacht salesperson, who worked for Yachts Ltd. His job was to take these yachts around to various different…
questions.text: Brendan was a yacht salesperson, who worked for Yachts Ltd. His job was to take these yachts around to various different marinas in the country and then show potential customers what a great time could be had by inviting potential customers onto the boat to experience the lifestyle that goes with a yacht. Brendan was given considerable control over what he could do so long as he made the sale. The only limitation that Yachts Ltd placed on their salesperson was that they were not allowed to use the yacht for their personal use but only to use the yacht for a potential sale. One week Brendan was at a marina, and he felt that business was very slow. He was trying to encourage people to take the boat for a test spin to see if they would be interested in buying it, but there were no takers. As the day drew to a close, Brendan had a brainwave. He decided that he would invite a number of his friends, a cool crowd of interesting people, to a party on the boat that night, hoping that people, seeing the fun that was being had on the yacht, would then decide to visit and arrange a test spin for the following day. As the party was in full swing, Bill dropped by and said he was interested in taking the yacht for a test spin. Brendan asked that he return the next day, but that tonight was for partying. Bill was disappointed and many of Brendan’s friends also began to encourage Brendan to take the yacht out for a spin. Eventually, Brendan relented and took the yacht out into the bay where it collided with another yacht due to Brendan’s fault. Advise Yachts Ltd if they are liable in tort law for the actions of Brendan under these circumstances.

questions.description: Critically evaluate the classification of damages in tort actions and in particular the role of punitive and exemplary d…
questions.text: Critically evaluate the classification of damages in tort actions and in particular the role of punitive and exemplary damages.

questions.description: Miriam owned Rover, a pet Labrador since he was a puppy. However, Miriam was unable to train Rover and as he grew, she f…
questions.text: Miriam owned Rover, a pet Labrador since he was a puppy. However, Miriam was unable to train Rover and as he grew, she found it more and more difficult to control him on the lead. Rover was a heavy and powerful dog which often led to Miriam being forced to drop his lead and leaving him to run free until he eventually came back of his own accord. Rover was well known in Miriam’s neighborhood as was Miriam’s inability to control him on the lead. One day while Miriam was taking Rover for a walk, Rover spotted a cat in one of the fields and he broke free from Miriam. He began chasing the cat onto the road. Fearful of Rover being hit by a passing car, Miriam began screaming for someone to help. Vanessa, who had been out jogging heard Miriam and saw Rover running into the road. Realising what had happened, Vanessa raced after Rover and began shouting at him, calling him to come to her. As Rover was still too interested in chasing the cat, Vanessas realised that the best thing she could do would be to lure Rover to her with a sweet that was in her back pocket. When Rover saw Vanessa with the sweet, he stopped, and the cat ran back into the field. Rover looked at the cat and then at Vanessa before deciding to head towards the sweet. As Rover ran toward Vanessa, he was struck by a car and seriously injured. Miriam has paid thousands of euros to the vet for Rover’s treatment. Advise Miriam if she has any action in the law of tort against Vanessa based on these facts.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law relating to liability in negligence for pure economic loss.
questions.text: Critically discuss the law relating to liability in negligence for pure economic loss.

questions.description: Jenny makes a skin care product from local, organically grown herbs and flowers, which she calls Organic Skin Refresher.…
questions.text:
Jenny makes a skin care product from local, organically grown herbs and flowers, which she calls Organic Skin Refresher. It is packaged in a pale green eco-friendly corrugated cardboard container. While Jenny wants to keep her business small and local, she is delighted that her product has been selected for sale in her local Megastore, a large retail chain with several branches nationwide. The idea is to showcase local producers in the locality where the store is located. Her product is highly successful and although she has been asked to make additional products such as hair shampoos and conditioners, she wants to remain small and local.
About six months after her products were placed on sale in Megastore, she noticed that right next to her products, Megastore have introduced a new range of hair care products under the name Organic Refresher. It uses the same pale green colour as her product and although the packaging looks identical to her eco-friendly corrugated cardboard, it is in fact made of non-recyclable plastic. It is on sale nationwide in all Megastores. Jenny is horrified that this product will be confused with her organic product. Advise Jenny if she has any action under the law relating to passing off against Megastore.

questions.description: Critically discuss the time limitation of actions relating to latent defects and suggest any reforms that you think migh…
questions.text: Critically discuss the time limitation of actions relating to latent defects and suggest any reforms that you think might be needed in this area.

questions.description: Frank was driving along the road when he came across road works. The signage was confusing and some of the bollards that…
questions.text: Frank was driving along the road when he came across road works. The signage was confusing and some of the bollards that had been put on the road to guide road users into the correct path have been blown into the wrong position by passing lorries. This should have been monitored by the road construction firm. As a result, Frank entered the wrong lane and as he continued down the road, he realised his mistake. Spotting an opportunity to correct his position, he began crossing over lanes to get back to where he should be. On one piece of the road where he was changing lanes, the road construction company had failed to put in a concrete barrier to prevent anybody entering it. As Frank went on to that section of the road, he got a puncture, which caused his front wheel to deflate very quickly. As his car ground to a halt in the wrong lane he was hit by Mary he was driving in her correct lane but had been distracted by her three children in the back seat of the car. Advised Frank who might be the cause of the accident in any tort action by Mary.

questions.description: Hilary is an alternative therapist who specialises in dealing with various different anxiety and depression issues using…
questions.text: Hilary is an alternative therapist who specialises in dealing with various different anxiety and depression issues using unconventional treatments. In particular, she uses what is known as an isolation room that is basically an empty room that is kept in complete and utter darkness. The idea simply put is to remove all external stimuli other than white noise. That sound is played at a volume that is barely perceptible to the human ear. Bill is a patient of Hilary. He is not responding well to the standard treatments for anxiety and depression. As a result, Hilary suggests that he try the isolation room. Bill is concerned that he will feel claustrophobic, but Hilary reassures him that he can ask to be released at any point. Bill agrees to try the isolation room. When Bill is in the room, Hilary starts the white noise. Bill begins to feel sick, and it later transpires that this is not an uncommon side effect of using white noise at that particular volume. Hilary was aware of this but had decided not to tell Bill since she believed the benefits outweighed the risks. Bill now starts shouting that he wishes to leave the room, but Hilary tells him that she will not release him until the time is over. After about 30 minutes, Hilary realizes that it is not working and lets Bill out. Advise Bill as to any action for an intentional tort that he may have against Hilary.

questions.description: Daily newspaper has published an op-ed piece by Gerry, about Eddie, a rural politician in Ireland. In the piece, Gerry h…
questions.text: Daily newspaper has published an op-ed piece by Gerry, about Eddie, a rural politician in Ireland. In the piece, Gerry has written the following: In my opinion - and this is just that one man's opinion - Eddie is unfit to be elected to government. In my opinion, Eddie appears to believe that Covid is not real, that the vaccinations are in attempt to microchip the population and that it is part of a new world order. Now Eddie has never said that, and to be fair it would be a valid viewpoint held by many people whom I admire and respect. My problem is that Eddie has never denied or accepted these allegations. In my opinion he should have the courage of his convictions and tell us all what he thinks. Otherwise, we are left with no option but to speculate. Now some might say that speculation is strengthened by the fact that he constantly attends demonstrations against the vaccination program, but I would not. I simply asked that he comes clean to the public he expects to vote for him. Eddie is shocked and horrified when he reads this. While it is true that he has attended anti-vaccination demonstrations, it has always been to argue for the vaccination program and debunk the microchip claim. He does, however, believe that the time has come for a single government for the world. Eddie further states that he has never been asked about the claims that Gerry is making. As a result, he has never been in a position to either deny or accept these allegations. Advise Eddie if he has any action in tort against Gerry for this op-ed piece and any defenses that may be available to Gerry.

questions.description: Molly is a senior manager with a large accountancy firm where she supervises over 30 people. As the workload has expande…
questions.text: Molly is a senior manager with a large accountancy firm where she supervises over 30 people. As the workload has expanded, Molly has become very stressed and many of her workers feel that she is aggressive and rude to them. One day Molly is in the middle of the office when one of her colleagues, Henry, starts arguing with her. Molly is furious at being humiliated in this way in front of her other colleagues. Finally, in a fit of anger Molly began shouting and screaming at Henry letting all the frustrations of the past erupt. Suddenly, Henry starts to clutch his chest and drops to the floor. All of Molly's colleagues gather round and start telling her that she has given Henry a heart attack. Molly is sick to her core and soon finds herself getting dizzy. She hears someone say Henry is dead. Molly now faints. When Molly wakes up, everyone is laughing, telling her that it was a practical joke to teach her a lesson. Henry says if she does not change her behavior, he could see someone having a real heart attack. Molly now cannot sleep at night, suffers panic attacks and finds she is unable to do her job. Advise Molly what action in tort, if any, she might have with respect to this situation.

questions.description: Critically discuss the liability in tort law for the acts of volunteers.
questions.text: Critically discuss the liability in tort law for the acts of volunteers.

questions.description: Jen operates an advice and support centre that helps small businesses in pursuing claims for non-payment of debts. Over …
questions.text: Jen operates an advice and support centre that helps small businesses in pursuing claims for non-payment of debts. Over the years she has become increasingly frustrated with the attitude of people with regard to paying her clients what they are owed. As a result, in recent times, she has taken to putting up posters in the front window of the centre which contain pictures and details of individuals who have been forced to court to pay the debts owed to her clients. On the posters she has a large color picture of the individual, with the words PAY YOUR DEBTS, SCUMBAG! Below in smaller print there is more detail about the amount of money owed, to whom and for how long. Jen calls it the window of infamy. One of Jen’s clients has accused Douglas of not paying what he owes to them. Douglas has disputed this, saying that he transferred the money electronically, although he admits that he may have provided incorrect bank details for Jen’s client but that his bank has said it may take some time to establish where the money has gone to, if they are ever able to find out. Douglas has refused to deal with the issue, saying it is for the bank to sort out. Jen decides to post Douglas’ picture in her window of infamy. One day, National Television is filming a piece on the impact of the pandemic for the evening news that night. When it is broadcast, the front of Jen’s centre is clearly visible and in particular the picture of Douglas with PAY YOUR DEBTS, SCUMBAG! is clearly visible. Advise Douglas if he has any action in tort law only against either Jen or National Television.

questions.description: Maurice is a surgeon specializing in the removal of varicose veins. Katherine has been referred to Maurice for bad varic…
questions.text: Maurice is a surgeon specializing in the removal of varicose veins. Katherine has been referred to Maurice for bad varicose veins on her left leg. After an examination, Maurice agreed that the varicose veins in her left leg were sufficiently bad that they needed to be surgically removed. He arranged an appointment for 6 weeks after this examination, as there was a waiting list for the operating theatre. When the day of the operation came, Katherine was wheeled into the operating theatre where she met Maurice who said to her “Now Katherine don’t worry, we will take good care of you. You will be under general anesthetic so you will not feel anything. I will do whatever needs to be done for that leg of yours. Trust me. Is that Ok?” Katherine nodded and replied “Maurice, that’s absolutely fine, I know you will only do what is necessary.” As the anesthetic was being given to Katherine, Maurice began looking at her right leg. He had not remembered seeing any varicose veins on her examination but now it was clear that her right leg would soon be as bad as her left leg. He tried to talk to Katherine, but she was already unconscious. He began operating on Katherine’s left leg and it went exceptionally well. At that point Maurice decided it would be a good idea to operate on her right leg as it would have to be operated on within a year in any event. The theatre nurse advised Maurice that they were only supposed to operate on her left leg, but Maurice had said that Katherine had said he could do whatever was necessary. The operation on both legs was a great success. However, Katherine was furious that Maurice had operated on leg without her consent. Advise Katherine if she has any action in tort against Maurice for the operation on her right leg.

questions.description: Alexa was an administrator working for WeHearYou Ltd., a company which provided call centre services, when the Covid-19 …
questions.text: Alexa was an administrator working for WeHearYou Ltd., a company which provided call centre services, when the Covid-19 pandemic resulted in the first lockdown and all employees were required to work from home. Every employee was given a specialised telephone and headset which enabled everyone to take customer calls from the comfort of their own home. Alexa set up her office in an upstairs bedroom and found that she was very productive. However, she noted that she was feeling pain in her lower back, which she put down to the use of a dining room chair while she was sitting at a dressing table where her laptop was located. She loved her chair back in the office but there was nothing to be done now. When the first lockdown ended, Alexa returned to working from the office and she emailed HR noting that if there was a second lockdown, she would like to take her office chair home with her. HR wrote back that they could not authorise that but in the event of a second lockdown the issue would be revisited. Six months later there was a second lockdown and all employees of WeHearYou Ltd were told to work from home. Alexa again asked HR could she take her office chair home with her and was told that as no decision had been made, she could not take the chair home at that point. About a week into the lockdown, Alexa suffered a severe spasm in her back as a result of poor posture and on the dining room chair, as she got up to walk it off, she tripped over trailing extension leads for her laptop and printer and broke her ankle. Advise WeHearYou Ltd what liability in tort law they might have to Alexa in these circumstances.

questions.description: Critically discuss how damages are calculated in a tort action, including but not limited to situations where the tort r…
questions.text: Critically discuss how damages are calculated in a tort action, including but not limited to situations where the tort results in the death of the plaintiff.

questions.description: John was working for a major finance company, MoneyBags Ltd. As John became more senior, he was given more authority to …
questions.text: John was working for a major finance company, MoneyBags Ltd. As John became more senior, he was given more authority to make financial trades online on his own and without approval. These trades involved buying and selling shares on behalf of clients and were worth many millions of euros. When the Covid-19 pandemic arose, John, like all his colleagues, was told to work from home and supplied with a secure laptop which was encrypted and had a secure internet link to the head office. In addition, MoneyBags Ltd provided instructions for working from home. Employees were to designate a room in their accommodation to which no one else was to have access, the computer was to be locked and password protected anytime it was left unattended and was not to be used by anyone other than the employee. John found working from home very difficult because the schools were closed, and his teenage son was at home at a loose end. His son would pound around the house, playing his music at a loud volume. As a result, John decided to use his bedroom to set up his laptop and work from there. One evening, John was called to the front door as one of his neighbours needed help. While he was doing this, his partner Saoirse was in their bedroom when she decided that she wanted to check on her FacePicture page. As she knew John’s password she logged on and in doing so accidentally sold 1 million shares belonging to Shane for only 1 cent each, when they were worth 1 euro each. Advise Shane as to any action he might have in the law of tort on these facts.

questions.description: Critically discuss the distinction between public and private nuisance, referring to relevant case law.
questions.text: Critically discuss the distinction between public and private nuisance, referring to relevant case law.

questions.description: The local authority had established a small pitch and putt course for the benefit of the local community. Over time howe…
questions.text: The local authority had established a small pitch and putt course for the benefit of the local community. Over time however, it had become clear that there was insufficient interest from the local community in playing pitch and putt and the costs of running it were becoming excessive for the possible benefit to the community. As a result, the local authority decided to close the course while it decided what to do with the land. In closing the course, the local authority simply locked the gates but maintained no on-site security, relying instead upon An Garda Siochana and their patrols. As a result, the course had become attractive to young people who found themselves with nothing to do during the winter nights and they would often climb over the fence and play on the course themselves, followed by heavy drinking sessions and music raves. Many in the community were annoyed at the way the local authority had abandoned the course and ignored the anti-social behaviour that was constantly taking place. As a result, Simon, a local resident, decided to take matters into his own hands. He hid on the course and waited for the first sign of these young people to arrive. When they did so, he ran at them shouting, attempting to chase them off. Suddenly he fell into a big hole which the local authority had dug for survey purposes. The hole had been covered with rotten wooden boards and Simon had been so angry he had not spotted the traffic cones at each of the four corners of the hole. Advise Simon what action if any he might have against the local authority.

questions.description: Critically discuss the issue of concurrent wrongdoers in tort law, paying particular attention to relevant jurisprudence…
questions.text: Critically discuss the issue of concurrent wrongdoers in tort law, paying particular attention to relevant jurisprudence in the allocation of responsibility between wrongdoers.

questions.description: The traditional view that intentional torts do not require harm is inconsistent with the primary policy goal in tort law…
questions.text: The traditional view that intentional torts do not require harm is inconsistent with the primary policy goal in tort law of compensating victims of wrongful actions. If the plaintiff has suffered no harm, there is no purpose to a tort action. Critically discuss this statement in the context of the law on intentional torts and their usefulness for plaintiffs.

questions.description: Jane was a junior surgeon who had shown great promise. In this hospital, she was allowed to operate on her own without s…
questions.text: Jane was a junior surgeon who had shown great promise. In this hospital, she was allowed to operate on her own without supervision, provided that the actual surgery was not so complicated that only a consultant surgeon would undertake the surgery in which case a junior surgeon could only observe. Unusually, this hospital had a practice that any decision was a judgement call by the junior surgeon based on the complexity of the operation and their personal skill and experience. One day Jane was undertaking a routine operation on her patient, Jim. During the course of that surgery, she unexpectedly came across a cancerous growth. After some consideration, Jane decided that she would take the opportunity to remove the growth. Although it was very close to a vital organ, Jane felt confident that she could handle it. Many surgeons would believe that Jane was wrong about that as the operation would be considered too complex for a junior surgeon. The theatre nurse was surprised because in other hospitals where she had worked previously, a junior surgeon would never conduct a procedure that they had not done before unless supervised by a consultant although this was not a standard requirement at this hospital. Nonetheless, the theatre nurse asked Jane if she should call for a consultant surgeon to take over but Jane said that it was not necessary. Jane told her that it needed to be done now and they could not wait until a consultant surgeon was available. Medically however, the majority of surgeons would disagree that it required immediate intervention. Jane successfully finished the operation, and all appeared well. However, several days later Jim was readmitted to hospital in agony: an infection had arisen where the cancerous growth had been removed. Advise Jim if Jane has breached the standard of care that she owed him while undertaking the operation.

questions.description: Dermot, a student, rented a house from Acme Homes under a short-term lease. One of the terms of the lease was that no mo…
questions.text:
Dermot, a student, rented a house from Acme Homes under a short-term lease. One of the terms of the lease was that no more than 6 people could be present in the house at any one time. The reason for this was twofold: one, to prevent noisy parties that might annoy the
neighbours and two, the internal stairs was a circular steel staircase built around a pole which was rated for a maximum weight of 400kg or 6 people. Dermot was sick and tired of the government’s pandemic restrictions which limited his social life considerably. He missed the night life and socialisation with his friends. Dermot decided to invite 10 of his friends to a small party and he bought in alcohol and food for a long night. On the night in question however, each of the people he invited also brought at least two other people with them. Dermot was getting worried that the party might attract attention from the police. Dermot then told everyone who was not invited to leave. However, the gate crashers became aggressive and refused to leave. Later that night, there was a competition to see how many people could get on the circular stairs. As the eighth person got on, the staircase collapsed, injuring two people who were not on the stairs: Pam, one of Dermot’s guests and Pam’s friend Jim, who had not been invited and had refused to leave when asked. Advise Pam and Jim if they have any action in tort law against Dermot.

questions.description: Amy operated a trampoline alley, which was simply a series of trampolines set out within a large warehouse. People eithe…
questions.text: Amy operated a trampoline alley, which was simply a series of trampolines set out within a large warehouse. People either individually or as part of a group would then pay to spend up to 30 minutes on the trampolines where they could exercise in fun. The trampolines had to be re-tensioned every morning before opening to ensure that they would provide sufficient ‘bounce’ to the participants. Amy was normally very careful about this, making sure not to provide either insufficient or excessive tension, but one morning she had been out late and as a result she over-tightened one of the trampolines, resulting in excess tension and increased ‘bounce’. Terry was a bodybuilder who liked every now and then to have some fun exercise and so that morning, he had booked a trampoline. Shortly after he began, he noticed that there was significant ‘bounce’ which he really enjoyed although he did feel it was getting risky jumping so high. Unfortunately, on his last bounce he went so high that when he came down he landed on the trampoline next to his which was being used by Gina. As a result, Gina fell off her trampoline and landed on the ground and was knocked unconscious. Terry was so distressed that despite Amy telling him not to move Gina and wait for the ambulance, he picked Gina up and drove her to the hospital in his car. All the movement caused Gina’s lung to be punctured from the broken rib she had suffered when she landed on the ground. Advise Gina as to who may be the cause of her injuries in a tort action.

questions.description: Joe owned a disused petrol station which he decided that he wanted to redevelop for apartments. Buried in the ground wer…
questions.text: Joe owned a disused petrol station which he decided that he wanted to redevelop for apartments. Buried in the ground were steel tanks which were used to store petrol when the station was in operation. The tanks were designed to last for over 100 years and were buried some 10 metres underground. When the petrol station was closed, the bulk of the petrol stored in these tanks was sucked out and taken away. However, it was always realised that notwithstanding this, there would remain a small but not insignificant amount of petroleum residue which would remain in the tanks. Joe was advised that it would be sufficient to concrete over the tanks and this would render them absolutely safe. After this was done and as Joe was building the apartments, the local authority was widening the road leading to his development. In order to do this, they had to blast some rocks that were obstructing the completion of the road. In a very controlled explosion, they blasted their way through the rocks and the road was completed. Some months later, Mary, a farmer whose land was adjacent to Joe’s property noticed the water from her [water] well had a foul oil-based smell and also contained other petroleum contaminants. An investigation concluded that the blast had caused damage to the buried tanks on Joe’s development and the contents of the tanks were now leaking into the water table. Advise Mary if she has any action in tort against Joe in these circumstances.

questions.description: Critically discuss the liability of volunteers in the law of tort, both under legislation and case law.
questions.text: Critically discuss the liability of volunteers in the law of tort, both under legislation and case law.

questions.description: Michael was a salesperson for CarsRus, a car dealership. He was working one Friday evening by himself when at around 4pm…
questions.text: Michael was a salesperson for CarsRus, a car dealership. He was working one Friday evening by himself when at around 4pm Angela dropped in and said that she was looking for a family minivan at a reasonable price. Michael had several vehicles to show her. Angela really liked one of the minivans that Michael had offered her and wanted to take it for a test drive. However, Angela did not have her driving licence with her and Michael was conscious that due to the pandemic, only one person was allowed in a vehicle at any one time. As a result of this, it was a strict policy of the dealership that no one could take a vehicle for a test drive without leaving a valid driving license. Angela seemed inclined to buy the car but was annoyed that Michael would not let her take it for a test drive and made that annoyance clear to Michael. As Michael was trying to explain to her that his hands were tied on this issue, Angela’s husband, Dwight, arrived at the dealership in a panic. He explained that their own car had broken down and that he would now miss his flight because he would not be able to get to the airport in time. Angela now also became very anxious and asked Michael if he could take them to the airport. Michael was hesitant but was also fearful of losing a sale, so he reluctantly agreed. When they went outside, he was surprised to see Angela’s two children also waiting. Undeterred Angela insisted that he take all of them to the airport and then demanded that they take the minivan she was interested in and that Angela could use the opportunity to test drive the vehicle. Again, Michael was reluctant, but the logic of Angela’s request was compelling. On the way to the airport, while Angela was driving, she injured a pedestrian, Kevin. Advise CarsRus as to their liability in tort, if any.

questions.description: Critically discuss the development of product liability paying particular attention to the introduction of the Liability…
questions.text: Critically discuss the development of product liability paying particular attention to the introduction of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 and relevant case law.

questions.description: Nico works in Associated Pills, a multinational firm that specializes in producing a pharmaceutical pill to protect agai…
questions.text: Nico works in Associated Pills, a multinational firm that specializes in producing a pharmaceutical pill to protect against the flu for export around the world. During the process of manufacture one of the raw materials must be heated to boiling point in specialised containers to ensure that any harmful micro-organisms are destroyed. Unfortunately, this means that Nico is forced to work where the temperature can often exceed 30 degrees Celsius, as he is required to continuously stir the containers in which the raw material is heated in. Nico is provided with gloves and an insulating suit to protect him from the heat but this has the practical impact of hindering his movement and slows down his work rate. He can work much quicker without the suit but it can become unbearably hot. Associated Pills mandates that the suit is worn at all times and that it is prohibited to enter the area without wearing both the suit and gloves. Nico however would usually wear the provided suit, despite the fact that the supervisors on the factory floor generally speaking did not tend to enforce this rule as other employees did not like wearing the suit. Recently, Associated Pills has decided to increase production, so they have put more containers in the same space until their new factory extension is complete. This is making movement in the area even more restricted. As a result Nico has taken to not wearing the suit provided at any stage. At the same time, his supervisor has become more active in the requirement that a suit be worn, and has told Nico several times that he should be wearing the suit. However, he has told them that in the new cramped conditions, the suit is more likely to cause an accident than protect him. One day, Nico collapses from heat exhaustion and is taken to hospital where he is told that he will not be able to work again as he has suffered irreversible damage from the hot conditions of his workplace. Advise Nico if he has any claim in tort law against Associated Pills.

questions.description: The use of damages in tort law is inappropriate in modern times in two ways. First, compensating victims through monetar…
questions.text: The use of damages in tort law is inappropriate in modern times in two ways. First, compensating victims through monetary payments is usually an inadequate form of redress and second, it often fails to act as a sufficient catalyst for change to prevent torts occurring in the first place. Critically discuss this statement.

questions.description: Tom and Harry have a long history of not liking each other, even though they often frequented the same pub. One night th…
questions.text: Tom and Harry have a long history of not liking each other, even though they often frequented the same pub. One night they were out having a drink in the pub with their respective friends when the owner of the pub announced that he was running a competition on his Bucking Bull  machine. This machine would simulate the situation where someone was sitting on top of a bull who was trying to throw the rider off. Whoever lasted the longest would win dinner for four. When Tom signed up, Harry also signed up saying ‘I am not going to let that fecker win!’ Each competitor had three rounds to see how long they would last. Tom and Harry were way ahead of everyone else by the end of the second round and so the competition was down to them alone. At that point, Harry decided that he desperately wanted to win, so after he had completed his turn, unknown to anyone, he turned up the speed on the Bucking Bull machine from Fast to Very Fast. His intention was solely to ensure Tom was thrown earlier than anyone else. When Tom got on the Bucking Bull, he was thrown violently from it, so violently that he landed outside the safety ring and fell on the hard floor, injuring his two legs. Suspecting that Harry had done something, Tom stood precariously in front of Harry and said ‘You are not leaving here tonight until you admit what you did!’ Harry replied ‘Out of my way!’ and brushed passed Tom heading towards the exit. Tom pushed Harry from behind who fell on top of Sarah who was carrying two pints back to her friend’s table. As Sarah fell the pint glasses shattered injuring Sarah. Critically discuss the potential liability of Tom and Harry, if any, in tort law only.

questions.description: Sam owns a small derelict house which he is in the process of renovating to live in. As he is doing the work by himself,…
questions.text: Sam owns a small derelict house which he is in the process of renovating to live in. As he is doing the work by himself, it is taking a long time. Although he has the site boarded up, people in the neighbourhood have found ways to get into the derelict house and often use it to hang out for drinking parties. Sam tries his best to stop this from happening but he feels it is a losing battle. One night, Mary and Ann are sneaking some drinks in the derelict house when a beam falls on Ann and pins her to the ground, knocking her unconscious. Mary is scared that she will be in trouble if she is caught, so she decided to leave Ann and anonymously call the Guards saying someone was inside the house, believing that they would get an ambulance. Although the Guards call to the house they can hear nothing and presume it is a prank call. However, several hours later, Sam, on his way home from a late night drinking session with some friends, decides to check in on the house. He finds Ann, who is now semi-conscious. Sam picks Ann up and puts her into his car and drives her to the hospital. On the way however, because Sam is over the alcohol limit, they are involved in a collision with another car. Eventually Ann arrives at the hospital. She is found to have suffered long term injuries from exposure which could have been avoided if she had gotten to the hospital earlier. In addition those injuries have been exacerbated by her being moved from the house without the proper equipment and also from the accident that she was in with Sam. Critically discuss who is the legal cause of Ann’s injuries from that night.

questions.description: Aileen owns an apartment in a large complex. A couple of years ago, the owners of the complex built a small shopping cen…
questions.text: Aileen owns an apartment in a large complex. A couple of years ago, the owners of the complex built a small shopping centre with an attached multi-storey car park next to her apartment. The multi-storey car park is of an open plan design. Aileen had no objections to the development in the beginning but soon after it opened, she found that the noise from the cars parking during the day was quite annoying. Not only was there the sound of the engines, but also the squeal of the tyres and brakes, the honking of the horns, the smell of the exhaust fumes and the general noise as people left and returned to their vehicles. One day, one of the parked vehicles caught fire but it was thankfully put out in time before any major damage was done, although the smell in Aileen’s apartment that time was horrendous. However what has really upset Aileen has been the decision of the company to offer overnight parking. This has resulted in a constant stream of cars coming and going throughout the night and not just during commercial hours. In the beginning the car park had 24/7 security but due to the recession, this has been reduced so that there is no security in place from midnight to 7am. As a result of this, some of the local teenagers are running through the car park in the middle of the night, pounding on the vehicles and setting of the alarms on the cars. They think it is hilarious but it keeps Aileen awake all night long. Aileen has lost patience and wants to close the car park down. Advise Aileen as to what action in tort, if any, she might have in these circumstances.

questions.description: It is often said that the liability of an employer for the acts of their employee must be within the scope of their empl…
