When Janice had agreed a price with Joey for the car, Joey asked would she take a 20 foot motorboat as part exchange. Janice was unsure, as they had never taken anything like this before, although they had taken things like motor homes and, she even remembered once having taken a mobile home in part-exchange. Still, she felt this was sufficiently unusual that she would have to get approval from Chandler. However, Chandler was not due back for four weeks. Joey was disappointed but he understood. However, he asked Janice if he could leave the boat parked on her sales lot while waiting for a decision, rather than driving it to his home. Janice hesitated but keen to give every chance to making this sale, she agreed. Joey left the boat at the front of the dealership. Some days later, Janice heard that there was violent storm headed towards the dealership, so she decided to tow the boat back to Joey’s house. Unfortunately, just as she had pushed the boat onto the side of the main road in preparation for hitching it to her truck, a gust of wind from the storm blew the boat onto a neighbouring property, causing significant damage. Advise Chandler if he is liable for the actions of Janice in any tort action that might arise as a result of the harm to the neighbours’ property.

questions.description: Critically discuss the liability in tort for the negligent discharge of both mandatory statutory obligations and discret…
questions.text: Critically discuss the liability in tort for the negligent discharge of both mandatory statutory obligations and discretionary powers by public authorities.

questions.description: Billy owns a public house in a small village in the countryside. For a variety of reasons his clientele has been steadil…
questions.text: Billy owns a public house in a small village in the countryside. For a variety of reasons his clientele has been steadily shrinking, So much so that he knows virtually all his regular clients by name. In an attempt to increase business in the pub, Billy has run a Valentine’s Night event. Essentially, whenever someone buys a drink, they will get another drink of the same value free if they can show they are celebrating Valentine’s night with a loved one. Word spreads through the town like wildfire and on Valentine’s night Billy’s pub is packed. Billy is taken aback. He had thought that the offer would be popular but had not anticipated the sheer numbers of people involved. Overwhelmed, Billy does not bother checking whether any individual ordering a drink has a date but simply puts up two drinks for every order received. He also does the same for Larry, a regular who is at the pub every night. By the end of the evening, Billy is exhausted and Larry is completely inebriated. As Billy begins clearing the pub, Larry asks if he can stay the night as he does not feel that he is in a fit condition to drive home. Billy is taken aback, and says “Go on a way out of that, you’re grand. You cannot stay here, that’s for sure.” As Larry gets up, he stumbles to the floor and again asks Billy if he can stay. Billy says “Sure listen, it’s only a couple of miles, you’ll be grand.” Larry asks Billy to keep his car keys until the morning as he says that he doesn’t trust himself to drive, but Billy does not want the responsibility and says “If you are able to know that you shouldn’t drive, you are not likely to drive, now are you.” Larry replies with “I suppose that’s true!” Larry loses control on the drive home and is severely injured. Advise Billy if he owes Larry a duty of care under the law of tort.

questions.description: Tom and Harry were in a pub watching a local football match on the big screen television. Tom supported the Kilmac Maule…
questions.text: Tom and Harry were in a pub watching a local football match on the big screen television. Tom supported the Kilmac Maulers and Harry supported the Ballyfun Tigers, two clubs with a long tradition of rivalry in sporting prowess on the field. As it was coming to an end, the Kilmac Maulers were in the lead by one point when the Ballyfun Tigers were awarded a penalty and from which they scored a goal. Tom shouted in disgust “Typical of those bloody Tigers, paid off the referee no doubt! That was no penalty”. Harry was incensed and shouted back “Come over here and say that you chicken! We have wiped your hides!” The whistle blew with the Tigers 2 points ahead. Harry was elated, and began singing loudly all around Tom about the various deficiencies of the people from Kilmac. As Tom began to leave, Harry kept blocking him, taunting Tom and shouting “Tiger’s rule, we mauled Kilmac!” No matter which way Tom turned, Harry moved to block him. “Stay here, chicken liver, face your demons! I am not letting you go from here until you go until you admit the Maulers are useless,” Tom became exasperated and shouted “Get out of my way, I am leaving now.” At that Harry picked up a glass bottle, smashed it on the table and said “Oh yeah: I would like to see you try.” Tom sat back down on the barstool at which point, Harry laughed hysterically “See how brave ‘Tombaleena’ is. Scaredee cat!” As Harry turned around to look for support from others in the bar, Tom punched Harry in his left kidney and Harry dropped like a stone. Tom then kicked Harry in the stomach as he lay on the ground writhing in agony. Advise Tom and Harry as to their potential liability in any tort action that might arise from these events.

questions.description: The law of tort relating to liability for animals in Ireland has developed in such a way as to reflect the changing natu…
questions.text: The law of tort relating to liability for animals in Ireland has developed in such a way as to reflect the changing nature of society from a predominantly agriculturally based community to an urbanised nation. Critically discuss this statement in light of relevant jurisprudence.

questions.description: Joyce really disliked Jennifer immensely.  There had been a history of bitterness between the individuals right the way …
questions.text: Joyce really disliked Jennifer immensely.  There had been a history of bitterness between the individuals right the way through Law School and into their practice as solicitors.  Each would try to outdo the other in terms of academic achievement, the size of their practice, etc. Jennifer was seeking to represent Dick, the editor of a widely circulated newspaper, who was currently involved in a high profile case about to go to court.  Joyce was incensed, and began to tweet the following #JenniferFail: “Seeking to represent a major client in a defamation case when she failed her tort exam” and then “Had to repeat and got the lowest pass grade possible. Epic JennFail!”  Followed up with “Has Jennifer ever won a court case in her career? Epic JennFail”. Jennifer was unaware of the tweets put out by Joyce so was surprised when Dick wrote to her formally and said the he was no longer interested in having her represent him as he felt that she did not match the profile of the sort of lawyer he would need and her firm was not sufficiently large enough to handle the case.  About a month later, Jennifer was made aware of Joyce’s tweets and Jennifer subsequently discovered that Dick was an avid tweeter and is convinced that Joyce’s tweets are responsible for his decision. Jennifer has in fact been later for her tort exam due to a major road crash and therefore had not failed the exam but had received an absent grade. Because she had to repeat it, her grade was capped at the lowest pass grade.  Jennifer has in fact won many court cases, although she has also lost a number of cases. Advise Jennifer if she has any action in tort as a result of the tweets from Joyce.

questions.description: Critically evaluate whether the jurisprudence on exemplary or punitive damages in Ireland is sufficiently robust so as t…
questions.text: Critically evaluate whether the jurisprudence on exemplary or punitive damages in Ireland is sufficiently robust so as to provide an effective mechanism for modifying the behaviour of unscrupulous defendants in tort actions.

questions.description: EdValu was a company whose primary task was to provide valuations on real estate located within Ireland.  EdValu had rec…
questions.text: EdValu was a company whose primary task was to provide valuations on real estate located within Ireland.  EdValu had recently received a lucrative contract from the National Unit Trust Society-an autonomous government agency which bought property in financial distress from various banks-to value all such property west of the Shannon. EdValu had been asked to provide a valuation to the National Unit Trust Society for certain lands in the greater Ducoyne townland that they were considering selling. EdValu made a site visit to the property, talked to locals, both potential purchasers and auctioneers, and as a result of this EdValu valued the land at a mere 1.2 million euro.  This was based on the fact that there would be little to no commercial interest in the property and that fundamentally it was only any use as farmland and even at the there was little interest in farm land in the Ducoyne area.  By accident, EdValu made this valuation known to a small number of people in the area. When the land went to tender only one tender was received, which was from a local auctioneer on behalf of a local syndicate of investors. The land was sold to them for the 1.3 million euro tender received, which was in excess of the valuation obtained for EdValu. Two months later, the property was re-sold to Handbook, a large US based Internet Company for 10 million euro as it exactly matched all their specific requirements.  The National Unit Trust Society is enraged.  They believe that EdValu caused them harm by leaking the valuation, albeit accidentally and also by failing to undertake a minimal amount research prospective commercial purchasers of the land in question since an internet search would have revealed that Handbook was looking for a rural site similar to the land in Ducoyne. Advise the National Unit Trust Society as to any potential tort action against EdValu in these circumstances.

questions.description: Sally and Harry are a newlywed couple who have just moved into a new housing estate.  The estate was destined to have 60…
questions.text: Sally and Harry are a newlywed couple who have just moved into a new housing estate.  The estate was destined to have 60 houses but unfortunately it is only partially completed and Sally and Harry live in one of only 6 occupied houses in the estate.  About another 20 houses are in various states of completion and the remainder consists of green field land. Across from the estate was a run-down supermarket but just before Sally and Harry moved in, it had been purchased by a major European supermarket chain, Xylo, who have created a huge hypermarket in its place. Initially, Sally and Harry were delighted with this as they felt that it might possibly result in their estate being completed or at least having more neighbours move in. Unfortunately, they have found that after 11pm a large number of Xylo delivery trucks use the Xylo rest stop area. This has a number of consequences.  There is continuous illumination through Harry and Sally’s bedroom window for most of the night caused by the headlights of the trucks.  There is significant slamming of truck doors, music and talking and in addition many of the drivers continuously run their engines while they are resting. Harry is not too disturbed but the noise or light but Sally is finding it very difficult to sleep and she is now pregnant, she fears that her newborn baby will also find it impossible to get any sleep.  Recently she had notices that other non-Xylo trucks have begun to use the rest area and the noise is increasing.  Xylo have argued that the rest stop is vital to the distribution system for their current 30 stores in the country and for the expansion plans to double that number in the next 2 years, creating an extra 600 jobs nationwide.  They deny any non Xylo trucks use the facility. None of the other residents wishes to complain and Sally believes that is because they fear that to do so will make it impossible to sell their houses to anyone else.  However, Sally can put up with it no longer and wants it to stop. Advise Sally as to what, if any, remedy in tort might be available in this situation.

questions.description: The Statute of Limitations Act 1957 and Statute of Limitations (Amendment) Act 1991 provide specific time limits within …
questions.text: The Statute of Limitations Act 1957 and Statute of Limitations (Amendment) Act 1991 provide specific time limits within which a tort action for personal injury must be commenced.  Critically discuss the rationale behind these Acts and whether sufficient provision, legislatively or judicially, has been made to counterbalance any perceived disadvantages particularly with respect to latent harm and discoverability with respect to the time accrual of an action.

questions.description: Seabourne Sailing Club (“SCC”) is a voluntary association created by individuals who wish to encourage greater use of ou…
questions.text: Seabourne Sailing Club (“SCC”) is a voluntary association created by individuals who wish to encourage greater use of our seas, lakes and rivers by everyone.  During the summer, SCC was running a sea kayaking course for children for 6 to 10 years of age.  The course was designed for absolute beginners and the kayaks were being supplied personally by the members of the sailing club.  A small fee of €20 was payable by each child simply to aid in fundraising for SCC. Matt was the organiser of the sea kayaking course and he became concerned about the varying quality of kayaks that the members of SCC were donating to the course.  He turned to the Kayaking Safety Institute, a voluntary trade association that provided safety certification for its members who manufactured kayaks.  As a favour, albeit somewhat reluctantly, the Kayaking Safety Institute agreed that they would look over the kayaks and warn Matt if any were no up to a sufficient standard. About a week before the course, the Kayaking Safety Institute visited Matt’s house and examined the kayaks, passing all of them as fit for duty.  However, they failed to ask if the kayaks would be used on the river or at sea and Matt never informed them of the intended use.  While all kayaks were fit for use on calmer river or lake locations, two were completely unsuitable for use at sea.  These kayaks sank on the first day of the course, causing injury to two of the children. The Kayaking Safety Institute is concerned that they may be exposed to a liability in tort as a result of these actions. Critically advise the Institute as to whether in action for negligence, they owe a duty of care to the injured children.

questions.description: Critically discuss the extent to which the rules on causation have operated in Irish courts to limit liability in tort. …
questions.text: Critically discuss the extent to which the rules on causation have operated in Irish courts to limit liability in tort.  To what extent has causation been a more effective limitation on liability in tort than the imposition of a duty of care?

questions.description: Henderson is the owner of Geddon, 100 acres of land which legend says contains the remains of a pre-historic settlement …
questions.text: Henderson is the owner of Geddon, 100 acres of land which legend says contains the remains of a pre-historic settlement buried deep underground.  As the hype concerning the alleged Mayan end of the world in 2012 prophecy begins to take hold, many have come to believe that Geddon is the one place which will provide safety on the fateful days to come and that if there is any hope of survival, it is to be on the sacred ground of Geddon. As a result many people have begun to gather close to Geddon in trailers, tents and caravans and they are causing an obstruction on the surrounding roads.  To ease this, Henderson has begun to allow people enter Geddon but has erected notices that they do so at their own risk.  The numbers of visitors has risen dramatically and Henderson notices that they have re-opened a dormant well to provide drinking water for themselves.  Henderson is aware that the well was closed as it was near collapse, but is of the view that it is of no concern to him as they are staying on his land for free.  In addition, he then posted notices around the well, stating that people drank the water at their own risk and that there was no guarantee that it was free from contamination. Henderson has now also begun going to Geddon daily, seeking a voluntary, ex gratia payment from those present in recognition of his generosity. If people refuse, he simply moves on. One day, a large group are gathered around the well.  Some are there to get water, while others are engaged in a ceremony in preparation for December 21, 2012. Suddenly the well collapses and a number of people are injured. Advise Henderson as to any potential liability in tort that he may owe those injured in these events.

questions.description: Gross Foods produces a potato crisp which is uniformly round in shape and also sold in cylindrical tins.  The tins are a…
questions.text: Gross Foods produces a potato crisp which is uniformly round in shape and also sold in cylindrical tins.  The tins are always wrapped in a plain black wrapper which contains no writing whatsoever except in small red print the words “Do it right.  Eat here”. It is a phenomenally successful product, not least of which is the fact that it is considered very trendy and fashionable to be seen with the product. Elite Camp Foods has decided to introduce a rice cake product which is uniformly round in shape and sold in cylindrical tins.  It has developed an innovative marketing programme for the introduction of this product based on the flavour combinations.  It will cover its product in a plain black wrapper which contains no writing whatsoever except in small red print the words “Were you right? Peel here”. The aim is to encourage customers to buy the product, taste it and then when finished try to guess which of the six new flavours it is and check whether they were right or wrong by peeling off the black wrapper to reveal the official wrapping underneath with the flavour. Elite Camp Foods were aware that the product was similar in packaging to that of Gross Foods, but as the promotion was temporary, did not believe that any harm would be done. Gross Foods have discovered that sales of their product dipped significantly during the period of the promotion and have not recovered fully. Advise Gross Foods as to any potential action in tort for these events.

questions.description: Detail the development of liability in tort for pure economic loss in Irish law.  Critically discuss the merit or otherw…
questions.text: Detail the development of liability in tort for pure economic loss in Irish law.  Critically discuss the merit or otherwise of these developments.

questions.description: Tom is a bus driver for Speedster Coaches working the Ballyduff to Kilmac route.  He is currently on the so called Red E…
questions.text: Tom is a bus driver for Speedster Coaches working the Ballyduff to Kilmac route.  He is currently on the so called Red Eye shift which leaves Ballyduff at 12 midnight for a 3am arrival at Kilmac. There are very few people on board the bus. However, one of the passengers, Jane, is from the same village as Tom and they have struck up a conversation.  Tom is quite interested in Jane and invites her to sit on the “Jump Seat”, a fold down seat at the front of the bus by the door and stairwell, designed for tour guides to sit while providing narration.  Company policy expressly prohibits the use of the “Jump Seat” at any time by anyone but Tom figures it will be fine as the bus is quiet and it is late at night. Towards the end of the journey, another passenger, Mary, asks Tom if he could take a short detour and drop her off at a small village just before Kilmac.  Tom knows that company policy prohibits any detour from the route but also knows that the drive to the village would be exceptionally scenic on this moonlit night and would impress Jane no end, so he agrees. On arrival at the village he opens the door, catching Jane unaware who falls from the “Jump Seat” and out of the bus, breaking her arm. Tom is in a panic.  He needs to get Jane to a hospital quickly so he remotely opens the luggage bay for Mary. Returning to the bus and with Jane moaning in the agony, he hurriedly presses the switch to close the luggage bay without checking that Mary has finished.  In fact she is still trying to get her suitcase out when the luggage bay door comes down on her back causing significantly injury. Speedster Coaches believe that Tom has not acted as a proper employee and do not feel that they should be liable to either Jane or Mary for Tom’s actions. Advise Speedster as to their potential liability in tort only.

questions.description: Harry bought a smartphone, the PearCom, from his friend Lisa who was emigrating.  However, as Harry was on a different n…
questions.text: Harry bought a smartphone, the PearCom, from his friend Lisa who was emigrating.  However, as Harry was on a different network, she had to contact her own network provider to cancel her mobile phone number and to have the phone unlocked so Harry could transfer his SIM. After a few weeks Harry began to notice that whenever he left the PearCom on paper, the paper would begin to turn yellowish and feel warm to the touch. He did not think that was that important and he continued to enjoy using the phone. In fact, PearCom has been quietly recalling a small number of their phones, contacting the original purchaser of the product by sending text messages to their mobile phone numbers, asking them to return to the store for a free replacement.  The text messages to Lisa of course never arrived. The problem was exacerbated if the phone was left charging in excess of 12 hours, although the instruction booklet expressly warned that the phone was never to be left on charge for more than 8 hours at a time. Only about a hundred or so phones in the early production run were affected and PearCom did not want to damage sales of their product through a public recall. Harry went away on business for a week, forgetting to take his PearCom and leaving it charging on his desk on top of a sheaf of papers. The PearCom overheated and set the paper alight, causing considerable fire damage to Harry’s office. Advise PearCom as to their potential statutory liability in these circumstances for the defective product.

questions.description: The tort of negligent infliction of nervous shock where a plaintiff has not suffered any physical harm is nothing more t…
questions.text: The tort of negligent infliction of nervous shock where a plaintiff has not suffered any physical harm is nothing more than a charter for marginally affected individuals to recover inappropriate compensation. Critically discuss this statement.

questions.description: William runs a small restaurant. At the back of the restaurant there is a waste plot of land owned by Richard, which has…
questions.text: William runs a small restaurant. At the back of the restaurant there is a waste plot of land owned by Richard, which has been left overgrown and disused. The restaurant and the waste plot of land are separated by a chain link fence with a very fine mesh wire. As business has fallen off, William has taken to looking for ways to reduce his costs.  Waste disposal costs are extremely high and so William decides that he will dispose of uncooked vegetables such like by throwing them into the waste plot of land there they will harmlessly decay back into nature. However, William then realises that the fine mesh wire will prevent all of the vegetable waste from being thrown over so he decides to hire Fred to remove the wire mesh, warning Fred not, under any circumstances, to step onto Richard’s property. William has been throwing his waste vegetables into Richard’s property for about three weeks when one evening Richard visits his property and sees what has been happening. Richard waits for William to come out of the restaurant and when he sees William approaching, begins to shout “Don’t you throw your rubbish onto my land!” William is initially startled but soon recovers and shouts back “It is waste land anyway and it isn’t doing any harm!” At which point William throws the contents of a bowl of gone off lettuce leaves over the chain link remnant of the fence, and onto Richard. Advise Richard if he has any claim in tort for the actions of William.

questions.description: Limitation of tort actions can be said to provide the certainty of ensuring actions are processed in a timely fashion wh…
questions.text: Limitation of tort actions can be said to provide the certainty of ensuring actions are processed in a timely fashion which strikes an appropriate balance between the competing interests of both litigants. Critically review this statement in light of Irish legislation and case law, paying particular attention to any provisions which aim to differentiate between the nature of the harm and the limitation period.

questions.description: Harry set up a website called “Rate my Piano Teacher” the basic thrust of which was to provide a mechanism whereby peopl…
questions.text: Harry set up a website called “Rate my Piano Teacher” the basic thrust of which was to provide a mechanism whereby people could share their experiences of piano teachers anonymously. The website makes it very clear that the views expressed on the site are personal to the contributor and not endorsed in any way by the website. If people wished to post a review of a piano teacher they had to complete an electronic form and submit it to Harry. The electronic form contained a number of questions and placed a word limit on the responses. Harry would never read these submissions since, once submitted, a computer programme would simply post them directly to the website. To both submit and access the reviews on the website, customers had to log on and validate their identity with a credit card, although Harry did not actually charge for the service.  He did however make money from the site through advertising. Nigel and Denis are rival piano teachers. Nigel has noticed that Denis has had an exceptionally high number of favourable reviews on the site.  Upon investigation, Nigel discovered that the majority of these reviews while being complimentary about Denis, also contained derogatory comments about Nigel, such as “Denis’ piano room is spotlessly clean without any sign of the vermin that infest Nigel’s” and “Denis has worked in top orchestras across Europe unlike Nigel who has mostly played in prisons across Europe.”  Finally, “Too often piano teachers, like Nigel for example, are simply in it for the money and care nothing about their students:  Denis is different from that sort of shyster”. Nigel now seeks your advice as to whether Harry has any liability in tort for these harmful statements which he believes are costing him potential students.

questions.description: Establishing a duty of care between the parties to a tort action now falls within set categories which it is in reality …
questions.text: Establishing a duty of care between the parties to a tort action now falls within set categories which it is in reality no longer possible to expand or alter to any significant extent.  Essentially the tort of negligence has grown up and now provides certainty through restricting the creation of any new duty of care. Critically discuss this statement paying particular attention to relevant Irish case law.

questions.description: BluBlood Engineering has recently been awarded the contract to lay a new water main in the Old Port area.  Old Port is b…
questions.text: BluBlood Engineering has recently been awarded the contract to lay a new water main in the Old Port area.  Old Port is built on very soft soils and has always had to battle issues with erosion and subsidence which required constant underpinning of the various houses and other structures in the area. Jack is a senior manager with BluBlood Engineering and is responsible for digging up a channel, laying down the water main and filling it in again on the main street of Old Port.  Work had progressed well, when Mary came to Jack very excited and upset.  It seems that while the excavation work was going on outside Mary’s house, the inside back wall of her house had begun to develop cracks. Jack went to investigate and knew they were only plaster cracks and that his firm would repair them free of charge. However, Mary was exceptionally distressed and she felt that BluBlood had been acting very poorly throughout this.  Although Jack had been specifically instructed not to offer anything to residents without first getting permission from his line manager, as a peace offering he promised Mary that he would fill in the potholes on her driveway with some spare tarmacadam. Jack returned later that evening when all the other workers were gone home and began working on Mary’s driveway.  Jack was unsure how to operate the machinery and lost control of it, destroying Mary’s car. Advise BluBlood Engineering if they have any liability in tort to Mary in these circumstances.

questions.description: Suzanne owns an airsoft rifle range.  Airsoft is a legal product which uses replica guns but fires a small 6mm round pla…
questions.text: Suzanne owns an airsoft rifle range.  Airsoft is a legal product which uses replica guns but fires a small 6mm round plastic pellet which is completely harmless even if hit at point blank range.  Even so the product is regulated and may only be used in authorised settings such as Suzanne’s rifle range.  There are very few such authorised ranges in the country. Suzanne has had considerable difficulty with her neighbour Frank who operates an organic egg hatchery.  First, Frank has complained that some of Suzanne’s customers are such bad shots that more than the occasional stray pellet crosses over into his hatchery.  The difficulty with this is that the chickens will eat the pellet and it causes immense digestive difficulties.  Second, the noise from the airsoft rifles tends to frighten the chickens and they are very difficult to settle. Suzanne had tried to deal with the first issue by erecting a higher fence with a smaller mesh and putting up signs that customers should aim towards the right of the range, which is away from Frank’s place. However, many of her customers are relatively poor shots and there is no fence with a small enough mesh to stop every 6mm pellet. Only a solid concrete wall could do that and Suzanne cannot afford it as the range does not generate enough business.  As regards the noise she has curtailed her opening hours to between 10am and 4pm. Frank is infuriated that despite Suzanne promising to do something about the problems, nothing has changed. He does not care if Suzanne goes out of business or if her customers have nowhere else to go. Advise Frank what action in tort, if any, might be available to him to get Suzanne to close the rifle range.

questions.description: Critically discuss the development of professional negligence in Irish law.  To what extent, if any, has the use of “sta…
questions.text: Critically discuss the development of professional negligence in Irish law.  To what extent, if any, has the use of “standard practice” rendered professionals immune from liability in negligence?

questions.description: Bernadette’s 16 year old son, Tony, was part of an activity summer camp located in the west of the country.  On the thir…
questions.text: Bernadette’s 16 year old son, Tony, was part of an activity summer camp located in the west of the country.  On the third day of the summer camp, Bernadette turned on the radio and heard an emergency news broadcast that a major disaster had occurred at the summer camp and that the area had been sealed off.  Fatalities were expected. Without a moment’s thought, Bernadette hopped into the car and drove to the summer camp as quickly as she could.  The place was sealed by An Garda Síochana but when she explained who she was, they allowed her in and told her to report to the camp manger, George. Bernadette was beside herself but managed to make contact with George and when she gave her name, George asked her to sit down.  He explained calmly and rationally that there had been an incident during a rock climbing activity and some of the boys had fallen and there had been some serious injuries.  He asked for her son’s name again and Bernadette repeated it.  George continued that Tony had been slightly injured and that she need not worry. Each of the boys had been placed in a separate cabin and being looked after by a doctor.  He asked if she wanted to see Tony and Bernadette said yes. George walked with Bernadette to Tony’s cabin and opening the door he stood back and let Bernadette enter. Suddenly Bernadette started screaming and shouting. When George looked in there was a dead body covered with a white cloth and priest saying the last rites.  George realised that he had taken her to the wrong cabin and Tony was fine in another cabin. Bernadette now suffers horrible nightmares and cannot sleep at night. Advise Bernadette if she has any action in tort against George for the events of that night.

questions.description: Michael owned a hotel which had run into financial difficulties due to the recession. Business had fallen off considerab…
questions.text: Michael owned a hotel which had run into financial difficulties due to the recession. Business had fallen off considerably and his costs were still rising. Worst of all, he faced stiff competition from a rival hotel on the other side of town which seemed to be able to make all sorts of bargain offers. Michael was at a loss to work out how they could afford to charge such reduced prices and still make any profit. Michael did some investigating and discovered that his three main suppliers, Fred, Sam and Ben all gave his rival an additional 25% discount.  Michael invited Fred, Sam and Ben to his hotel for a drink.  Michael ushered them into a medium sized room where in the centre there was a table, four chairs and nothing else. Suddenly the door closed behind Michael. Fred, Sam and Ben could hear what they thought was the door locking.  Michael reached down, picked up a hurley and began absentmindedly twirling it between his hands. He asked why he wasn‘t getting any discount.  Fred said that he wasn’t putting up with this and began making his way to the door. In a quiet voice, Michael said “That’s one option”. Fred stopped and sat down scared. Ben then said that Michael wasn’t buying enough to qualify for a discount. Michael let the hurley fall to the ground with a loud bang startling Ben.  Ben sat down as well.  Sam then suggested that Michael was free to do business elsewhere if he could get a better deal.  Again in a quiet voice Michael said that the three might never be able to do any business anymore unless this was resolved.  All three were now exceptionally scared and felt that they could not leave. They agreed to give Michael the same discount. As they finished, Fred asked if they were free to leave.  Michael said they could have left at any time they wanted. As they got up to go, Michael pushed them out the door saying “You won’t try that again - now get out.” Advise Fred, Ben and Sam if they have any action in tort for Michael’s behaviour during this meeting.

questions.description: Critically evaluate the tort of passing off in modern Irish tort law.
questions.text: Critically evaluate the tort of passing off in modern Irish tort law.

questions.description: During a recent spell of exceptionally icy weather, Eddie noticed that the public footpath outside his house had become …
questions.text: During a recent spell of exceptionally icy weather, Eddie noticed that the public footpath outside his house had become very slippery.  Eddie had a perverse sense of humour and would often take to sitting in his front room looking out onto the footpath and waiting for people to slip.  He thought that this was hilarious.  When he told his friends about this, they said that he should tape it and post the video on UsVid, a free internet site created for these sort of funny home videos. Eddie began to notice that some of the best falls were happening at night, but this meant that the video was of poor quality due to the lack of light.  Eddie decided to install a small spotlight to illuminate the footpath outside his house so that the video would be clearer.  One evening, Eddie was in hysterics as loads of people were slipping and sliding, when he noticed his elderly neighbour Ellen approaching.  Edie did not think watching Ellen falling would be funny so he switched off the video camera and spotlight and went to the kitchen to make tea. When he returned he was shocked to see Ellen lying on the footpath motionless but decided against trying to help Ellen. Instead he waited for fifteen minutes until another pedestrian came across Ellen and called for an ambulance.  Ellen suffered hypothermia that might have been avoided had Eddie called for an ambulance when he first saw Ellen on the footpath. Advise Ellen as to any potential liability in tort that he might owe to Ellen.

questions.description: Jonathon had his car serviced by Acme Car Technicians in preparation for the annual car test.  This service involved a 1…
questions.text: Jonathon had his car serviced by Acme Car Technicians in preparation for the annual car test.  This service involved a 125 point inspection which included a simulated car test. During the simulation, Jonathon’s car was put on the rolling road test.  Due to a malfunction the speed limiter on the rolling road machine was not operative as a result of which the wheels were turning 40 kph faster than they should have been.  Ordinarily this would not have been a problem but the wheel nuts on the front tyre had not been properly tightened by Jonathon following a flat tyre the previous day.  As a result, the front wheel flew off, shattering the viewing window of the garage. Startled by the loud noise, Alan, a mechanic with Acme Car Technicians, failed to engage the handbrake fully on another car he was working on with the result that it rolled forward out onto the street and crashed into Mary’s car which was parked outside. Mary, who had left her 6 month old baby in the car while she popped into the newsagent, was extremely distraught when she saw what happened and this only got worse when she found she couldn’t open the door of the car as she crash had caused the central locking system to activate incorrectly. Advise Acme Car Technicians as to their legal responsibility for these events.

questions.description: Critically discuss the developments and impact of the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur in Irish law.
questions.text: Critically discuss the developments and impact of the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur in Irish law.

questions.description: Cyprian was a star player on the local rugby team.  Over the years a tension had developed with a rival team player, Ber…
questions.text: Cyprian was a star player on the local rugby team.  Over the years a tension had developed with a rival team player, Bertie, with each of them goading the other both on and off the pitch. During the course of the country final against Bertie’s team, Cyprian was constantly making unsavoury remarks about Bertie, pulling at his jersey when the referee wasn’t looking and similar niggling tactics. Bertie was getting more enraged with each passing moment and the last straw was when Cyprian accused Bertie of driving a “girlie car”.  Bertie bided his time and in the middle of a play, Bertie flung himself at Cyprian, bringing him to the ground.  The referee did not call it as a foul but blew the whistle for a different infringement.  As Cyprian was lying on the ground writhing in agony, Bertie grabbed some earth and stuffed it in Cyprian’s mouth, saying: “How do you like it now?”  Bertie ran off leaving Cyprian behind.  In fact Bertie had broken Cyprian’s leg in two places from the tackle and had a chipped tooth from the earth being stuffed in his mouth. Cyprian was taken to hospital and while he was there he was shown a video of the match where it was clear that Bertie had committed a foul, although not one which would have warranted being sent off, when he broke Cyprian’s leg.  Barite later told Cyprian that while he had intended “to take Cyprian down” in the tackle and teach him a lesson, he had never meant to break his leg. Advise Cyprian as to any tort action that might be available to him against Bertie in these circumstances.

questions.description: CO2 Drinks have purchased the Rainbow directly from Roadster Ltd.  The Rainbow is an industrial machine designed to put …
questions.text: CO2 Drinks have purchased the Rainbow directly from Roadster Ltd.  The Rainbow is an industrial machine designed to put caps on glass bottles containing carbonated drinks.  The Rainbow is based on an old design but one which has been substantially modified by Roadster. In particular, Roadster has made certain modifications to the application column designed to speed up the operation of the Rainbow.  Subsequent testing however has found that the modified column is subject to cracking as the bolts used are about 25mm too short.  All customers are informed of this and have been asked to either stop using the machine until an engineer can call to replace the bolts or replace the bolts themselves to the relevant specifications following which Roadster engineers will call to ensure that the new bolts meet specifications. In order to avoid stopping production, CO2 Drinks has decided to replace the bolts themselves and have dismantled the application column.  Unfortunately the only have 4 of the 6 bolts required which meet the specifications. Believing it would be safer to at least have 4 of the bolts at the correct length than none, CO2 Drinks contacted Roadster to check if this was permissible.  Roadster had never come across this but could see no reason not to proceed with changing only 4 of the 6 bolts and changing the remaining two later. Unfortunately, no one knew that the differing bolt lengths would cause excessive shearing force and lead to the catastrophic failure of the application column within a matter of days. There was considerable property damage to the CO2 Drinks factory as a result of this incident. Advise Roadster of any potential tort liability for the defect in the Roadster Rainbow.

questions.description: Critically evaluate the different types of damages that may be awarded in a tort action paying particular attention to t…
questions.text: Critically evaluate the different types of damages that may be awarded in a tort action paying particular attention to the underlying purpose of these classifications.

questions.description: John believes in the old Mayan prophecies that the world will go through major upheavals in December 2012.  With that in…
questions.text: John believes in the old Mayan prophecies that the world will go through major upheavals in December 2012.  With that in mind, he has bought five acres of hillside in a remote area and has begun to make preparations for the turmoil that is to come.  John has two neighbours, Fred and Harry. As John makes preparations for him and his family, he realises that a secure water supply is essential and begins to create a small dam where he can divert an existing stream on his land into a large artificial pond which he hopes will be sufficient.  In addition, he has installed an underground 500 litre petrol tank to supply his energy needs and a wood burning furnace for his heating requirements. During a bad storm, the pond burst its banks and flooded both John and Fred’s land.  It was during the cleanup of this flooding that John noticed that his underground petrol tank was overflowing.  John realised that during the flooding, rainwater had gotten into the petrol tank causing it to overflow. In the meantime, Fred found that his house was now flooding from the overflow from John’s pond.  This had never happened before.  Worse, Fred also discovered that his sheep were getting sick from drinking the water which was contaminated with petrol.  Meanwhile Harry has discovered that John’s use of the wood burning furnace is resulting in a high level of carcinogens blowing onto Harry’s land and this is causing breathing difficulties for him and his family. Advise John as to the potential liability in tort, if any, arising from his action.

questions.description: Lionel has a social website which he uses to post personal information about his life, his views on the world, what he h…
questions.text: Lionel has a social website which he uses to post personal information about his life, his views on the world, what he has been doing recently and so forth.  He restricts access to his friends only, by requiring them to register and use a password to access the site.  In the beginning, he only allows access by people personally known to him but he now accepts requests for access if an existing member will vouch for the individual.  At the moment, there are about 30 registered users who have full access to the site. Jennifer was a friend of Lionel from an internet chat room whom he had never physically met.  Lionel registered Jennifer for access to his site but since Jennifer did not know any other registered user of the site and didn’t particularly want to, she never visited it.  Lionel and Jennifer were getting on very well and Lionel asked Jennifer to meet up and go on a date.  Jennifer said that she would consider it but Lionel needed to send his picture to her first.  After she received his picture, she emailed saying that she didn’t think their relationship was going anywhere. Lionel was enraged.  He removed Jennifer’s access from his website and posted a long rant against Jennifer, saying that she looked nothing like the picture she used on her website and that she should stop lying about her prior ‘modelling’ experience as it was clear she was not model material.  Last week, Jennifer received an email from Mary, a registered user of Lionel’s website, sympathising with Jennifer and saying that Jennifer should be happy with the way she looks and not try to pretend otherwise. Jennifer was horrified.  Although the website picture is of Jennifer, it was taken about 4 years ago.  While most of the modelling experience that she refers to on her website is true, she has embellished certain parts of her experience.  She is concerned that she will lose potential work opportunities. Advise Jennifer as to any tort action she may have in this situation against Lionel.

questions.description: Outline the basis for tort liability with respect to injuries caused by animals and critically discuss whether the rules…
questions.text: Outline the basis for tort liability with respect to injuries caused by animals and critically discuss whether the rules provide a coherent and logical framework for such liability at the present time.

questions.description: The common areas of a large suburban housing estate are under the control of a management company, Esta Services Ltd.  E…
questions.text: The common areas of a large suburban housing estate are under the control of a management company, Esta Services Ltd.  Esta Services has received many complaints from residents within the estate about people driving too quickly through the estate given the large number of children who play there.  Originally Esta Services try to monitor the situation with security patrols and ask for help from the Gardaí but to no avail. Accordingly they decide to put in speed ramps throughout the estate.  The process is partially completed in that the speed ramps have been installed, but Esta Services discovers that the warning signs for these ramps are incorrect.  Instead of “Caution: Speed Ramps Ahead” the sign reads “Caution: Speed Bamps ahead”.  Rather than put these signs up, they send them back to the supplier for correction. When Esta Services went to paint the speed ramps in a reflective warning colour, they discovered that the speed ramps were 10cm too high.  Rather than paint them they decided to save money and wait until they were reduced in height by the company which installed them. Gerry lives in the estate but has been overseas on a work contract for the last six months.  His flight home arrived at 2.30am after a long delay.  During the drive home, Gerry received a call from his alarm monitoring company alerting him to the fact that his house appeared to have been broken into and that the Gardaí had been called. Gerry now raced home quicker than he would have, hoping to scare off the intruders.  As he drove through his estate he hit one of the ramps at speed.  The ensuing jolt caused Gerry to hit his head off the roof of the car, knocking him out.  His car then veered off course and slammed into a tree.  He suffered serious injuries. Advise Esta Services as to their potential liability in tort, if any.

questions.description: Monica was eight months pregnant when she suffered severe abdominal pains and immediately went to General Hospital.  On …
questions.text: Monica was eight months pregnant when she suffered severe abdominal pains and immediately went to General Hospital.  On admission she was given an ultrasound scan but all appeared to be normal.  The pains subsided and she was discharged. Two days later, Monica woke in the middle of the night with more abdominal pains and her husband Mike called for an ambulance to take her to the hospital.  When Monica arrived, Claire, the nurse in charge, telephoned Jim, the doctor on call, and he advised that an ultrasound scan should be taken.  If that proved clear, then she would be admitted for the night and made as comfortable as possible and they would reassess her in the morning.  The ultrasound showed that all was still normal and so Monica was admitted for the night.  Again the pains subsided so that by the morning Monica discharged herself and apologised for being such a bother. The following night, the same thing happened.  When the nurse telephoned Jim again, he was very annoyed and shouted at Claire that she should be experienced enough at this stage to repeat what she had done the previous night and did not need to continually wake him unless there was a real emergency.  He also said that in these situations, the job of a nurse is to reassure patients who might be panicking. Two nights later Monica again suffered severe abdominal pains.  When Mike rang for an ambulance to take her to the hospital he was told that no ambulance was available due to the cutbacks.  Mike drove her to the hospital himself. On arrival Claire was the admitting nurse.  Thinking back on what Jim had said, Claire, contrary to hospital policy, decided not to call Jim and instead put Monica on a trolley and tried to make her comfortable.  This time the pains did not subside but got worse.  By the time Claire called Jim early the following morning, Monica’s baby was suffering from acute distress which led to subsequent serious complications. Advise General Hospital as to their potential liability in tort for the actions of Jim and Claire.

questions.description: Critically demonstrate the role of consent in an action for an international tort. Analyse the difficulties that arise w…
questions.text: Critically demonstrate the role of consent in an action for an international tort. Analyse the difficulties that arise with ensuring both the validity and scope of the consent given.

questions.description: Roger runs a family restaurant in downtown Ballykinsella.  He has been contacted by Terrence about booking the restauran…
questions.text: Roger runs a family restaurant in downtown Ballykinsella.  He has been contacted by Terrence about booking the restaurant for a party that he is throwing, subject to certain conditions.  The conditions are that the restaurant would be closed to the public for the day and that the food would be cooked exactly as specified. Roger agrees to this and Terence then presents Roger with his dining requirements.  First, there can be no animal or animal related products (other than eggs) in anything which is served to them as his guests believe that eating animal products will lead to their eternal damnation.  Second, at no point should any of their food be heated to boiling point or beyond as otherwise Terrence and his friends will not be taken when their Maker returns to Earth for them.  Roger is surprised but does not think that these conditions will cause too much difficulty. On the day of the party, all is going well at the restaurant until one of the guests starts screaming that she can taste bacon fat on her mushrooms.  Suddenly people are screaming everywhere, with many of them fainting and throwing up.  Several ambulances arrive and most of the guests end up in the Accident and Emergency Department.  Although most are released within a few hours, a significant number return in the next few days suffering from salmonella poisoning. On investigation, it is discovered that the mushrooms did have traces of bacon fat although no one can establish how it got there.  Moreover, scientifically there is no reason to throw up after eating bacon fat.  The salmonella was contracted because the eggs had not been cooked long enough to destroy the salmonella when they could not be boiled. Advise Roger as to whether under tort law he is responsible for these events.

questions.description: Sean is an independent financial advisor whose business involves preparing reports and gathering external valuations, et…
questions.text: Sean is an independent financial advisor whose business involves preparing reports and gathering external valuations, etc on the viability of large commercial investment loans for high net worth individuals.  These reports are used by banks to make loan decisions. Noel is a particularly valuable client for Sean who wants to borrow €1 million to invest in a proposed casino development which had been delayed for a number of years.  Noel presented Sean with a business plan for the investment, much to Sean’s relief.  Sean was never very good at these things and always appreciated a client who would undertake a lot of the preparatory work.  However, in the current climate he was conscious that he should undertake some independent checks of his own.  Most of it seemed to be taken from publicly available records and Sean called the auctioneer to confirm that he had given a valuation on the property.  The auctioneer was out of the office but his secretary confirmed that the valuation had been undertaken by them.  Sean put his name to Noel’s report making it look as if he had written it. The following day Bernard, who was thinking of becoming one of Sean’s clients, was in Sean’s office when he saw the report and read quickly through it.  Sean caught him doing this and told Bernard it was confidential.  Bernard asked Sean’s personal opinion as to whether it was a good investment.  All Sean would confirm was that with this report, the bank would in all probability approve the loan. Bernard went away and invested €1 million in the project without any further research. In fact, the auctioneer’s valuation was undertaken in 2007 before the recession and the bank turned Noel’s loan application down on the basis that the business plan was unrealistic. Advise Bernard, who has just discovered that the project closed with the loss of his entire investment, as to whether he has any potential remedy in tort against Sean.

questions.description: Private nuisance as an action in tort is of doubtful benefit to the ordinary litigant.  Outline the basic requirements t…
questions.text: Private nuisance as an action in tort is of doubtful benefit to the ordinary litigant.  Outline the basic requirements to establish an action in private nuisance and critically analyse these requirements with respect to the above statement.

questions.description: Jenny works for Head Ltd which sells homeopathic remedies based on ancient traditions. Until about 18 months ago, busine…
questions.text: Jenny works for Head Ltd which sells homeopathic remedies based on ancient traditions. Until about 18 months ago, business was rather sedate, mostly from the older generation who were looking for remedies for certain ailments such as arthritis. However in recent times, another group of customers has emerged who have realised that these herbs can also be used to alter their mood in a manner similar to alcohol and drugs. As word spread, the clientele became very different: far more aggressive and Jenny began to feel unsafe. In particular, Jenny had complained to her manager that customers were now hanging around the back entrance of the shop when she was putting out the rubbish from the shop and were scaring her. Her manager said that she should simply toughen as they were now making lots of money but he did promise to put in some CCTV cameras, which he duly did. Nonetheless she still felt unsafe and the CCTV cameras seemed not to be acting as a deterrent. One day Jenny was so scared that when she came back in she never locked the door behind her. Keith, seeing his chance, followed her in and stole an amount of herbs from the stockroom. Jenny’s manager was incensed and introduced a self-closing and locking door to the back which could only be opened by way of a key. Keith was arrested and sent to prison for 3 months. One day during that three month period, Jenny went out the back entrance but forgot her key. When she tried to get back in and could not, some of the people outside beat here severely. Advise Jenny if she has any claim in tort against Head Ltd.

questions.description: Jack was driving home from a nightclub last one night when he came across a car which had gone off the road and collided…
questions.text: Jack was driving home from a nightclub last one night when he came across a car which had gone off the road and collided with a tree. As the road was quite narrow and twisty, Jack stopped his car, put on his hazard flashers and went to see if there was anyone hurt. Inside the vehicle he saw Diane unconscious at the wheel, there was blood over her face. Jack quickly felt for Diane’s pulse and noticed that in addition to a strong heartbeat, her breathing seemed regular and strong. Jack got his mobile phone and tried to call 999 but there was no reception. Jack was now beginning to panic. He was frightened that another car might come along and crash into his car. Jack was also a first year medical student and he remembered a recent lecture indicating that most trauma victims fully recover from their injuries if they receive medical treatment within half an hour of the trauma event. Jack now decided to act swiftly. He carefully lifted Diane out of the vehicle and carried her to his car. He gently turned his car around and headed off to the local hospital. Arriving at the Accident and Emergency he noticed that it was in the darkness. He quickly saw the sign that due to cutbacks this A & E was closed after 8pm each night and directing people to a different hospital ten miles in the opposite direction, back the way he had come. Jack headed back eventually arriving at the A & E department. The doctors examined Diane and found that what would have been moderate injuries from the accident had been exacerbated by either Diane’s removal from the scene of the accident without proper neck support or the extended drive to the hospital. Diane is now unable to move her body from her neck down. Advise Jack as to any potential liability in tort he might owe Diane.

questions.description: Sam is an old age pensioner who lives in a large house in an isolated country area. His house is on the side of a hill a…
questions.text: Sam is an old age pensioner who lives in a large house in an isolated country area. His house is on the side of a hill and set well in from the road but he has a nice brick path leading from his gate to the front door. During recent unusually cold weather with very low temperatures, this path often froze over making it very icy. Several times the postman has complained to Sam about just how dangerous the path was but Sam just kept saying that there was nothing he could do and that this cold snap was highly unusual. One night, Sam was watching a television programme talking about the lack of salt to grit the roads to prevent ice forming. Sam thinks this is a great idea and begins to spread table salt over his path in preparation for the postman the following morning. The ice begins to melt almost instantly and Sam was very pleased. However, as the temperature continues to drop the melted ice re-freezes. Around midnight, while Sam was asleep, William was driving past Sam’s house when his car broke down. Seeing that there was a light on in Sam’s house, William decided that he would see if he could get help. While William was walking up the path he noticed that it was very slippery. William was about to get off the path when he slipped, sliding down a steep embankment which had been obscured by some hedging. As a result of the fall, William broke his leg and could not move. Despite calling at the top of his voice no one came to his aid. The postman found William the next morning suffering from severe frostbite. Advise William of any potential claim in tort against Sam for these events.

questions.description: The distinction between causation and remoteness is one that is neither helpful nor desirable in our legal system as the…
questions.text: The distinction between causation and remoteness is one that is neither helpful nor desirable in our legal system as they are really the same question asked in a different form. Critically discuss this statement referring to relevant authority.

questions.description: The Hillclimber is a large 4 wheel drive (4WD or AWD) SUV made by Caprica Motors Ltd., and which is normally sold to far…
questions.text: The Hillclimber is a large 4 wheel drive (4WD or AWD) SUV made by Caprica Motors Ltd., and which is normally sold to farmers and other people who engage in serious off road driving. However, it has become somewhat of a status symbol for city drivers and Caprica Motors have brought out a cheaper two wheel drive version (2WD). Bill recently purchased a new two wheel drive Hillclimber, although he knows nothing about cars. Bill read the manual which said that “in icy or snowy conditions, press the switch marked AWD to activate drive to all four wheels for secure effortless and safe driving in any condition.*” Bill glanced down at the * at the bottom of the page where it stated “Does not apply to 2WD variants. Always exercise caution when driving in adverse conditions.” But had no idea what 2WD meant so ignored it. All 2WD vehicles had the AWD switch but it served no purpose. It was retained simply to save costs. Caprica Motors instructed local dealers to place a warning sticker next to the AWD switch. The sticker on Bill’s car consisted of 4WD in red letters, surrounded by a red circle with a red line going through it. Bill had seen this warning sticker but did not understand the letters 4WD. One day in early January, Bill came across a line of cars unable to go up a hill due to icy conditions. Switching on the AWD, he accelerated past them, driving smoothly up the hill. Suddenly he lost control on the ice and spun out, damaging several cars before sliding back down the hill where he slammed into a wall. Bill believes that the Hillclimber did not operate as it should have. Advise Bill as to whether the Caprica Motors owe any liability in tort under statute.

questions.description: Ellen is a financial investment salesperson who works on her own and relies on commission earned from her sales. Ellen h…
questions.text: Ellen is a financial investment salesperson who works on her own and relies on commission earned from her sales. Ellen has a regular client list which she normally calls on an annual basis, but in recent times she has taken new clients given the downturn in the economy. On Friday Ellen received a telephone call from a recent client, Philip, asking her to meet him at his house urgently. Philip told her that he had come into a large sum of money that he was seeking to invest. When Ellen arrived at Philip’s house later that night, he opened the door and brought her into the living room. Ellen opened her briefcase expecting to show Philip some new investment opportunities when suddenly Philip flew into a temper and began shouting and screaming at her, saying that he had lost his life savings as a result of following her advice. He said that he wanted his money back right now and wanted a cheque for €40000. Ellen felt scared, and got up to go at which point Philip also stood up and said “You need to think twice before leaving this room without giving me my money!” Ellen sat down scared. Philip continued to pace around the room explaining how much money he had lost, what it meant to him and how he had to make her pay to ‘even things up’. As Ellen became increasingly more scared, she resolved to leave immediately. She stood up and began to walk towards the door. Philip was now shouting loudly as he moved to intercept her exit, saying “Oh you want to go eh? Let me open the door and leave you scurry off like the vermin you are!” Ellen panicked and swung her bag at Philip hitting him in the jaw. Unfortunately Ellen’s laptop was in her bag and Philip’s jaw was broken in three places. Ellen has now received a claim from Philip for the injuries he suffered. Advise Ellen as to her potential legal liability in tort law.

questions.text: Critically discuss the concept of vicarious liability. To what extent do you think it appropriate that an employer is made liable for the autonomous acts of an employee?
